w_S = 0.3,\; w_P = 0.3$. **Step 4 — Compute the weighted composite TBL score.** $$TBL_t \;=\; 0.4 \cdot \tilde{E}_t \;+\; 0.3 \cdot \tilde{S}_t \;+\; 0.3 \cdot \tilde{P}_t$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation scale (standard KTU rubric).** | Range of $TBL_t$ | Performance Band | |---|---| | $0.00 - 0.33$ | Unsustainable | | $0.34 - 0.66$ | Transitional | | $0.67 - 1.00$ | Sustainable | --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) A factory has the following data for two years: | Parameter | Year 1 | Year 2 | |---|---|---| | Emissions $E$ (kg CO₂-eq) | 8000 | 5000 | | Social Index $S$ (0–100) | 60 | 75 | | Operating Profit $P$ (₹ lakh) | 100 | 130 | Bounds: $E_{min} = 0,\; E_{max} = 10000$; $S_{min} = 0,\; S_{max} = 100$; $P_{min} = 0,\; P_{max} = 200$. **Year 1:** $$\tilde{E}_1 = \frac{10000 - 8000}{10000 - 0} = 0.20$$ $$\tilde{S}_1 = \frac{60 - 0}{100 - 0} = 0.60$$ $$\tilde{P}_1 = \frac{100 - 0}{200 - 0} = 0.50$$ $$TBL_1 = 0.4(0.20) + 0.3(0.60) + 0.3(0.50) = 0.08 + 0.18 + 0.15 = 0.41 \;(\text{Transitional})$$ **Year 2:** $$\tilde{E}_2 = \frac{10000 - 5000}{10000 - 0} = 0.50$$ $$\tilde{S}_2 = \frac{75 - 0}{100 - 0} = 0.75$$ $$\tilde{P}_2 = \frac{130 - 0}{200 - 0} = 0.65$$ $$TBL_2 = 0.4(0.50) + 0.3(0.75) + 0.3(0.65) = 0.20 + 0.225 + 0.195 = 0.62 \;(\text{Transitional, near sustainable})$$ **Conclusion:** The firm has improved from $0.41$ to $0.62$ — a +51% movement toward sustainability, with the largest gain coming from emissions reduction (Planet pillar), validating the sustainability strategy. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How TBL Links to Manufacturing Functions | Manufacturing Function | People Impact | Planet Impact | Profit Impact | |---|---|---|---| | Raw Material Selection | Worker exposure to toxics ↓ | Virgin resource use ↓ | Long-term cost ↓ | | Process Design | Automation safety ↑ | Energy & waste ↓ | Throughput ↑ | | Logistics | Driver welfare ↑ | Fuel emissions ↓ | Fuel cost ↓ | | End-of-Life | Community health ↑ | Landfill waste ↓ | Residual value ↑ | | Product Design | Ergonomic benefits ↑ | Recyclability ↑ | Market differentiation ↑ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — TBL Conceptual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Triple Bottom Line
Framework] --> B[People
Social Pillar] A --> C[Planet
Environmental Pillar] A --> D[Profit
Economic Pillar] B --> B1[Worker Safety] B --> B2[Fair Wages] B --> B3[Community CSR] B --> B4[Skill Development] C --> C1[Energy Efficiency] C --> C2[Emission Control] C --> C3[Waste Minimisation] C --> C4[Water Stewardship] D --> D1[Cost Reduction] D --> D2[Market Premium] D --> D3[Long-term ROI] D --> D4[Risk Mitigation] B1 --> E[Sustainable
Manufacturing Output] B2 --> E B3 --> E B4 --> E C1 --> E C2 --> E C3 --> E C4 --> E D1 --> E D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[UN SDG 12
Responsible Production] E --> G[Long-term
Enterprise Survival] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — TBL Feedback Loop in Manufacturing ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Profit Investment] --> P2[Green Technology] P2 --> P3[Lower Emissions] P3 --> P4[Healthier Workforce] P4 --> P5[Happier People] P5 --> P6[Higher Productivity] P6 --> P7[Higher Profit] P7 --> P1 P3 --> ENV[Planet Benefit] P5 --> SOC[People Benefit] P7 --> ECO[Profit Benefit] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams use only **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `P1`, `B1`, `ENV`) and **plain uppercase text labels** — no markdown formatting or reserved keywords inside the labels — to ensure clean Mermaid compilation. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (when physical drawings are infeasible) ```mermaid flowchart TB IN[Inputs:
Energy, Materials, Labour, Capital] --> PROC[Manufacturing Process] PROC --> OUT1[Outputs:
Product + Emissions + Waste + Heat] OUT1 --> PPL[People Pillar
Assessment] OUT1 --> PLT[Planet Pillar
Assessment] OUT1 --> PRF[Profit Pillar
Assessment] PPL --> TBL[TBL Composite Score] PLT --> TBL PRF --> TBL TBL --> DEC[Strategic Decisions:
Invest / Reform / Discontinue] DEC --> IN ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Triple Bottom Line. Name its three pillars.** **Model Answer:** The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, coined by John Elkington (1994), is a sustainability accounting framework that measures a firm's performance across three dimensions: **People (social equity)**, **Planet (environmental integrity)**, and **Profit (economic viability)**. It expands the conventional single financial bottom line to capture the full impact of an organisation on society and the environment. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between the traditional Bottom Line and the Triple Bottom Line.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Traditional Bottom Line | Triple Bottom Line | |---|---|---| | Focus | Profit only | Profit + People + Planet | | Stakeholders | Shareholders | Shareholders + society + environment | | Reporting | Annual financial statement | Integrated annual report (ESG) | | Time Horizon | Quarterly / annual | Multi-decade (inter-generational) | | Origin | Friedman doctrine (1970) | Elkington (1994) | --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q (a)** Explain in detail the concept of the Triple Bottom Line. Discuss how each of its three pillars can be operationalised in a manufacturing enterprise using suitable KPIs. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, introduced by **John Elkington** in 1994, is a holistic sustainability framework that requires a company to measure and report its performance across three pillars — **People, Planet, and Profit** — instead of focusing solely on financial returns. **Operationalisation in Manufacturing:** **1. People (Social Pillar):** - Occupational Health & Safety — measured via **LTIFR (Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate)**. - Wage fairness — ratio of lowest wage to local living wage. - Skill development — training hours per employee per year. - Diversity — % of women in technical roles. **2. Planet (Environmental Pillar):** - **Carbon intensity** = kg CO₂-eq per kg of product. - **Specific energy consumption** = kWh per unit. - **Water footprint** = m³ fresh water per unit. - **Waste recycling rate** = % of total waste diverted from landfill. - **Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)** as defined by Ellen MacArthur Foundation. **3. Profit (Economic Pillar):** - Long-term economic value added (EVA). - Life-cycle costing instead of purchase-cost focus. - Risk-adjusted return on capital employed (ROCE). - Revenue from sustainable product lines (% of total turnover). A sustainable manufacturer integrates these KPIs into its **Balanced Scorecard** so that trade-offs are made transparent. --- **Q (b)** A manufacturing unit has the following data: - Emissions: 6000 kg CO₂-eq (range 0–10000) - Social Index: 80 (range 0–100) - Profit: ₹150 lakh (range 0–200) - Weights: $w_E = 0.5, w_S = 0.25, w_P = 0.25$ Compute the TBL composite score and comment on the firm's sustainability band. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Normalise each pillar: $$\tilde{E} = \frac{10000 - 6000}{10000 - 0} = 0.40$$ $$\tilde{S} = \frac{80 - 0}{100 - 0} = 0.80$$ $$\tilde{P} = \frac{150 - 0}{200 - 0} = 0.75$$ Apply weights: $$TBL = 0.5(0.40) + 0.25(0.80) + 0.25(0.75)$$ $$TBL = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.1875 = 0.5875 \approx 0.59$$ **Comment:** Since $0.59$ lies in the range $0.34–0.66$, the firm is in the **Transitional band** — heading toward sustainability but not yet there. The dominant weakness is the environmental pillar, which alone accounts for only 0.20 of the total. Management should invest in cleaner production technologies to raise $\tilde{E}$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q (a)** "Profit is no longer the sole bottom line of a responsible manufacturer." Discuss this statement in the context of the Triple Bottom Line, with relevant examples from the Indian manufacturing sector. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The statement reflects the core philosophy of TBL that **sustained profit is a *consequence* of — not a substitute for — social and environmental responsibility**. **Supporting points:** 1. **Regulatory Pressure:** Indian Companies Act 2013, Section 135 mandates CSR spending of 2% of average net profits for eligible companies — a *legal* enforcement of the People pillar. 2. **Market Premium:** Studies by McKinsey (2022) show that 60% of Indian consumers prefer brands with verified ESG credentials, allowing a 5–15% price premium (Planet + Profit linkage). 3. **Risk Mitigation:** Factories near the Ganges (e.g., tanneries) that ignored the Planet pillar faced closure under NGT orders, wiping out decades of accumulated Profit. 4. **Talent Attraction:** Gen-Z workers in Kerala's manufacturing hubs (e.g., Kochi, Coimbatore cluster) rank employer sustainability reputation in their top-3 job-selection criteria. Hence, **profit is the *outcome* of balancing People + Planet + Profit**, not the singular goal. --- **Q (b)** A factory achieved the following TBL normalised scores: $\tilde{E} = 0.55, \tilde{S} = 0.40, \tilde{P} = 0.85$. Management proposes three alternative weight sets: - Set 1: $w_E = 0.6, w_S = 0.2, w_P = 0.2$ - Set 2: $w_E = 0.2, w_S = 0.6, w_P = 0.2$ - Set 3: $w_E = 0.33, w_S = 0.33, w_P = 0.33$ Compute the composite TBL under each set and recommend which best reflects a balanced sustainability philosophy. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Set 1 (Planet-dominant):** $$TBL_1 = 0.6(0.55) + 0.2(0.40) + 0.2(0.85) = 0.330 + 0.080 + 0.170 = 0.580$$ **Set 2 (People-dominant):** $$TBL_2 = 0.2(0.55) + 0.6(0.40) + 0.2(0.85) = 0.110 + 0.240 + 0.170 = 0.520$$ **Set 3 (Balanced):** $$TBL_3 = 0.333(0.55) + 0.333(0.40) + 0.333(0.85) = 0.183 + 0.133 + 0.283 = 0.599$$ **Recommendation:** **Set 3** is the *most balanced* because it reflects Elkington's original spirit — equal consideration of all three pillars. Even though Set 1 yields a numerically higher score ($0.580$), it is heavily skewed toward Planet and would mislead strategic decision-making by *masking* the weak social score ($0.40$). A balanced weight-set exposes the genuine weakest pillar and is the KTU-preferred philosophy. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Listing the three pillars without defining them individually — **−2 marks**. > 2. Treating TBL as a *trade-off* (e.g., "profit must be sacrificed for environment") instead of as *mutually reinforcing* — **−3 marks**. > 3. Failing to attribute the framework to **John Elkington (1994)** — **−1 mark**. > 4. In numericals, forgetting to **normalise** raw values before weighting — **−4 marks**. > 5. Using a single weight (e.g., $w_E = 1$) and ignoring the others — **−2 marks** (violates the constraint $\sum w_i = 1$). > 6. Confusing **Triple Bottom Line** with **Triple Bottom Line Accounting (TBLA)** — TBLA is the *act of measuring*; TBL is the *concept*. Examiners deduct **−1 mark** if these are used interchangeably. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TBL = People + Planet + Profit**, coined by **John Elkington (1994)**. - TBL is a **sustainability accounting framework**, NOT a slogan. - The three pillars are **mutually reinforcing**, not competing. - In manufacturing, each pillar must be tied to a **measurable KPI** (LTIFR, carbon intensity, EVA, etc.). - The composite TBL score uses **normalised values** in $[0,1]$ and **weights summing to 1**. - Bands: **$0.00–0.33$ Unsustainable**, **$0.34–0.66$ Transitional**, **$0.67–1.00$ Sustainable**. - TBL aligns with **UN SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption & Production)** and Indian **Companies Act 2013, Section 135**. - Standard reporting frameworks aligned to TBL: **GRI, ISO 14001, ISO 45001, CII GreenCo**. - Common Indian regulatory anchors for TBL: **National Green Tribunal (NGT)**, **CPCB emissions norms**, **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)**. - In 2018, Elkington himself called for **"a recall of TBL"** — not because the concept is wrong, but because it has been *misused as a balancing act*; the new demand is for **regenerative** outcomes where all three pillars *grow* simultaneously. - **Always show three distinct KPIs** in KTU answers — one per pillar — to score full marks on 7-mark theory questions.

topics.title: Environmental, Economic and Social Dimensions of Sustainability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT631 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Environmental, Economic and Social Dimensions of Sustainability # Environmental, Economic and Social Dimensions of Sustainability ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition **Sustainability** is formally defined (per the *Brundtland Report, 1987*) as: > *"Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* The concept is operationalised through **three interdependent pillars** — often called the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — coined by **John Elkington (1994)**. Each pillar must be balanced to achieve genuine sustainability: 1. **Environmental Dimension (Planet)** — ecological integrity, resource conservation, pollution prevention. 2. **Economic Dimension (Prosperity)** — financial viability, value creation, economic resilience. 3. **Social Dimension (People)** — social equity, human rights, community well-being. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PESFT631 Module 1 explicitly requires students to *"understand the three pillars of sustainability and their interlinkages in a manufacturing context."* Examiners expect students to cite **Elkington's Triple Bottom Line** and the **Brundtland definition** verbatim for full marks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three-Legged Stool Imagine a **three-legged wooden stool**. Each leg represents one dimension of sustainability: - **Environmental leg** → the planet's ecosystems and natural capital. - **Economic leg** → the financial engine that powers industry and jobs. - **Social leg** → the human community that gives purpose to production. If **even one leg is short, weak, or broken**, the stool collapses — no matter how strong the other two legs are. This is the central insight: a manufacturer may be highly profitable (strong economic leg) and eco-friendly (strong environmental leg), but if it exploits labour (broken social leg), the entire system becomes unsustainable. Conversely, environmentally-conscious and socially-fair production is meaningless if it is **economically unviable** and the firm goes bankrupt. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Manufacturing Analogy:** Consider an apparel factory in Bangladesh. It may produce cheap garments (economic ✓), but if it discharges untreated dye effluents into rivers (environmental ✗) and employs child labour (social ✗), the operation is **fundamentally unsustainable** — exactly the case with the *Rana Plaza collapse (2013)* which killed 1,134 workers. ### 1.3 The Sustainability Venn Diagram (Conceptual) At the intersection of all three dimensions lies the **sweet spot** often called **"Strong Sustainability"** or **"True Sustainability"** — the operating zone every manufacturer should aim for. | Region of the Venn Diagram | Real-World Manufacturing Example | |---|---| | Economic ∩ Environmental only | Green luxury brands that are profitable but use exploitative supply chains | | Economic ∩ Social only | Fair-trade cooperatives that are ethical but pollute local waterways | | Environmental ∩ Social only | Eco-villages that are self-sufficient but economically fragile | | **All Three (Center)** | **Circular economy manufacturers using renewable energy, fair wages, and closed-loop recycling** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pillar 1 — The Environmental Dimension (Planet) The environmental dimension concerns the **biosphere's capacity** to support industrial activity indefinitely. It focuses on five sub-systems: - **Atmosphere** — greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, air quality (PM2.5, SOx, NOx). - **Hydrosphere** — water withdrawal, effluent discharge, eutrophication. - **Lithosphere** — raw material extraction, mining, land-use change. - **Biosphere** — biodiversity loss, deforestation, habitat fragmentation. - **Technosphere** — solid waste, hazardous waste, end-of-life products. ### Key Environmental Metrics **1. Carbon Footprint (CF)** $$ CF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} AD_i \times EF_i $$ Where: - $AD_i$ = Activity Data for emission source $i$ (e.g., kWh of electricity, litres of fuel) - $EF_i$ = Emission Factor for source $i$ (e.g., **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh** for the Indian grid average — CEA 2023) **2. Ecological Footprint (EF)** $$ EF = \sum_{j} \frac{C_j}{P_j} \times EQF_j $$ Where: - $C_j$ = Consumption of item $j$ - $P_j$ = Annual productivity of the bioproductive land type - $EQF_j$ = Equivalence Factor converting to global hectares (gha) **3. Water Footprint (WF) — Hoekstra et al., 2011** $$ WF = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey} $$ - $WF_{blue}$ = volume of surface/groundwater consumed - $WF_{green}$ = rainwater consumed - $WF_{grey}$ = volume required to dilute pollutants to acceptable standards ## 2.2 Pillar 2 — The Economic Dimension (Prosperity) The economic dimension evaluates whether a manufacturing process is **financially viable over the long term**. It is **not** limited to short-term profit; it includes: - **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** — total cost of ownership from cradle to grave. - **Externalities** — costs imposed on society (e.g., pollution-related healthcare). - **Resource Efficiency** — value generated per unit of input. - **Decoupling** — economic growth that does not proportionally increase environmental pressure. ### Key Economic Metrics **1. Life-Cycle Costing (LCC)** $$ LCC = C_{capex} + C_{opex} + C_{maint} + C_{end\text{-}of\text{-}life} - C_{residual} $$ **2. Material Productivity (MP)** — also called *resource productivity* $$ MP = \frac{\text{Gross Value Added (GVA)}}{\text{Direct Material Input (DMI)}} $$ **3. External Cost (Environmental Externality)** $$ E_{cost} = \sum_{i} E_i \times P_i $$ Where $E_i$ = emissions of pollutant $i$ and $P_i$ = monetised damage cost per kg (e.g., **$0.05–$0.20 per kg CO₂** under various carbon-pricing schemes). ## 2.3 Pillar 3 — The Social Dimension (People) The social pillar is often the **least quantified** but most ethically critical dimension. It addresses: - **Labour Rights** — fair wages, safe working conditions, no child/forced labour. - **Health & Safety** — workplace accidents, occupational diseases. - **Community Impact** — local employment, indigenous rights, cultural heritage. - **Stakeholder Engagement** — transparency, grievance mechanisms. - **Product Responsibility** — consumer safety, ethical marketing. ### Key Social Metrics **1. Social Return on Investment (SROI)** $$ SROI = \frac{\text{Present Value of Social Benefits}}{\text{Present Value of Investments}} $$ **2. Gini Coefficient (Inequality Measure)** $$ G = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(2i - n - 1) \cdot x_i}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i} $$ Where $x_i$ is the cumulative income of population $i$, sorted in ascending order. **3. Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** $$ LTIFR = \frac{\text{Number of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Person-Hours Worked}} $$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | KTU Exam Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | Carbon Footprint | $\sum AD_i \cdot EF_i$ | kg CO₂e | Frequently asked (Part A) | | Ecological Footprint | $\sum \frac{C_j}{P_j} \cdot EQF_j$ | global hectares (gha) | Conceptual question | | Water Footprint | $WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | m³ / unit | Part A / Part B | | Life-Cycle Cost | $C_{capex} + C_{opex} + C_{maint} + C_{EOL} - C_{residual}$ | INR / USD | Full 14-mark question | | Material Productivity | $\text{GVA} / \text{DMI}$ | INR/kg | Frequently asked | | SROI | PV(Social Benefits) / PV(Investment) | Ratio | Theory question | | LTIFR | $\text{Injuries} \times 10^6 / \text{Person-Hours}$ | per million hrs | Part A definition | | Gini Coefficient | $\frac{\sum (2i - n - 1) x_i}{n \sum x_i}$ | 0 (equality) – 1 (max inequality) | Theory | | Triple Bottom Line | $S = f(Econ, Env, Soc)$ | Composite index | Core Module 1 | | Planetary Boundaries | 9 thresholds (Rockström, 2009) | Quantitative limits | Conceptual | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked about sustainability dimensions, *always* state that the three pillars are **mutually dependent**, not independent. A violation in one pillar compromises the others — this nuance earns the extra mark. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Brundtland Definition to the Triple Bottom Line The transition from the abstract Brundtland definition to the operational **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework involves four logical steps. ### Step 1 — Start with the Brundtland Equation The Brundtland definition is expressed mathematically as: $$ W_{future} \geq W_{present} $$ Where $W$ denotes the **welfare function** of the corresponding generation. The constraint says today's consumption must not erode tomorrow's welfare. ### Step 2 — Decompose Welfare into Three Components $$ W = f(E, N, S) $$ Where: - $E$ = Economic welfare (income, employment, goods/services) - $N$ = Natural capital (ecosystem services, biodiversity) - $S$ = Social welfare (health, equity, education) ### Step 3 — Apply the Substitutability Question Two schools of thought emerge: - **Weak Sustainability** assumes $E$, $N$, $S$ are *substitutable* — natural capital can be replaced by built capital (e.g., a factory replacing a forest). - **Strong Sustainability** assumes $N$ (natural capital) is *non-substitutable* — certain ecological thresholds cannot be crossed regardless of economic compensation. ### Step 4 — Operationalise via TBL Elkington (1994) translated this into the **Triple Bottom Line**: $$ TBL = TBL_{economic} + TBL_{environmental} + TBL_{social} $$ For a manufacturing firm, each component is measured by: $$ \begin{aligned} TBL_{economic} &= \text{Profit} + \text{Taxes Paid} + \text{Employment Generated} \\ TBL_{environmental} &= -(\text{Emissions}) - (\text{Waste}) - (\text{Resource Depletion}) \\ TBL_{social} &= +(\text{Fair Wages}) + (\text{Community Investment}) - (\text{Workplace Injuries}) \end{aligned} $$ > The **net TBL > 0** condition defines a sustainable firm. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating LCC of a CNC Machine A manufacturing firm is evaluating a CNC machine with the following data: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Capital cost ($C_{capex}$) | ₹ 50,00,000 | | Annual operating cost ($C_{opex}$) | ₹ 4,00,000 | | Annual maintenance ($C_{maint}$) | ₹ 1,50,000 | | End-of-life disposal ($C_{EOL}$) | ₹ 2,00,000 | | Residual scrap value ($C_{residual}$) | ₹ 5,00,000 | | Project life (n) | 10 years | | Discount rate (r) | 8% | ### Step 1 — Calculate Present Value of Recurring Costs $$ PV_{opex} = C_{opex} \times \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} PV_{opex} &= 4,00,000 \times \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} \\ &= 4,00,000 \times \frac{1 - 0.4632}{0.08} \\ &= 4,00,000 \times \frac{0.5368}{0.08} \\ &= 4,00,000 \times 6.7101 \\ &= \text{₹ } 26,84,040 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Calculate PV of Maintenance $$ \begin{aligned} PV_{maint} &= 1,50,000 \times 6.7101 \\ &= \text{₹ } 10,06,515 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Total LCC $$ \begin{aligned} LCC &= C_{capex} + PV_{opex} + PV_{maint} + C_{EOL} - C_{residual} \\ LCC &= 50,00,000 + 26,84,040 + 10,06,515 + 2,00,000 - 5,00,000 \\ LCC &= \text{₹ } 83,90,555 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The ₹ 50 Lakh purchase decision actually costs ₹ 83.9 Lakhs in today's money over 10 years. Sustainable procurement decisions require this **full LCC perspective** — never just the sticker price. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sustainability Dimensions in Manufacturing | Sub-Criterion | Environmental Indicator | Economic Indicator | Social Indicator | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy** | kWh / unit produced | Energy cost / unit | Worker heat-stress incidents | | **Materials** | kg virgin input / unit | ₹ material cost / unit | Supplier labour audits | | **Water** | L effluent / unit | Water tariff paid | Community water rights | | **Waste** | % recycled / reused | Disposal cost saved | Informal-sector recycler livelihoods | | **Emissions** | kg CO₂e / unit | Carbon credit revenue | Public health (asthma cases nearby) | | **Labour** | Eco-training hours | Wage / productivity ratio | Lost-time injury rate (LTIFR) | | **Product End-of-Life** | Design for Disassembly score | Buy-back revenue | Right-to-repair compliance | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three Pillars of Sustainability — Conceptual Map ```mermaid graph TD A[SUSTAINABILITY] --> B[ENVIRONMENTAL PILLAR Planet] A --> C[ECONOMIC PILLAR Prosperity] A --> D[SOCIAL PILLAR People] A --> E[STRONG SUSTAINABILITY Intersection] B --> B1[Climate & GHG Emissions] B --> B2[Water & Effluents] B --> B3[Materials & Circularity] B --> B4[Biodiversity & Land Use] C --> C1[Profitability & ROI] C --> C2[Life-Cycle Costing LCC] C --> C3[Resource Productivity] C --> C4[Externalities & Carbon Pricing] D --> D1[Labour Rights & Wages] D --> D2[Health & Safety LTIFR] D --> D3[Community Engagement] D --> D4[Stakeholder Transparency] B1 --> E B2 --> E B3 --> E B4 --> E C1 --> E C2 --> E C3 --> E C4 --> E D1 --> E D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#388e3c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Dimensions Interact in a Manufacturing Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT[Raw Material Inputs] --> PROD[Production Process] PROD --> OUTPUT[Products Outputs] PROD --> EMISSIONS[Emissions Waste Effluents] ENV[Environmental Dimension] -.monitors.-> INPUT ENV -.monitors.-> PROD ENV -.monitors.-> EMISSIONS ECO[Economic Dimension] -.controls.-> INPUT ECO -.controls.-> PROD ECO -.monitors.-> OUTPUT SOC[Social Dimension] -.governs.-> INPUT SOC -.governs.-> PROD SOC -.governs.-> OUTPUT STAKE[Stakeholders Community Workers Regulators Consumers] <--> SOC MARKET[Market Customers Investors] <--> ECO ECOSYS[Ecosystems Atmosphere Hydrosphere] <--> ENV PROD --> REPORT[Sustainability Report ESG Disclosure] style ENV fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style ECO fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#000,color:#000 style SOC fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000 style REPORT fill:#90caf9,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Evaluating Sustainability of a Manufacturing Process ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start: New Process Proposal]) --> Q1{Is it
Economically Viable?} Q1 -->|No| R1[Reject Process
Not Sustainable] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is it
Environmentally Safe?} Q2 -->|No| R2[Modify to Reduce
Environmental Impact] Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is it
Socially Acceptable?} R2 --> Q2 Q3 -->|No| R3[Modify to Improve
Social Outcomes] R3 --> Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| APPROVE[Approve as
Sustainable Process] APPROVE --> MON[Continuous Monitoring
and ESG Reporting] MON --> S style S fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff style APPROVE fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style R1 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff style R2 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff style R3 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff style MON fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *"Define the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) concept. Explain any two of its pillars with a manufacturing example each."* **Model Answer:** The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** is a sustainability framework introduced by **John Elkington (1994)** that extends the traditional reporting focus from economic profit to include environmental and social performance. It is often summarised as **"People, Planet, Prosperity"**. **(i) Environmental Pillar (Planet):** This pillar measures a firm's impact on natural systems. Example: A steel manufacturer installing **effluent treatment plants (ETP)** and **bag filters** to reduce SOx and particulate emissions, and switching to **electric arc furnaces powered by solar energy** to cut CO₂ emissions. **(ii) Economic Pillar (Prosperity):** This pillar measures financial viability and value creation. Example: A textile firm adopting **waterless dyeing technology**, which reduces water cost by **90%** while maintaining profitability. **(iii) Social Pillar (People):** This pillar measures impact on workers and community. Example: A garment factory providing **fair wages, crèche facilities for women workers, and regular health check-ups**, thereby improving worker retention and reducing strikes. A firm achieves **true sustainability only when all three pillars are simultaneously satisfied**. --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] *"Distinguish between Weak Sustainability and Strong Sustainability."* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Weak Sustainability | Strong Sustainability | |---|---|---| | **Core belief** | Natural capital and built capital are *substitutable* | Natural capital is *non-substitutable* | | **Replacement allowed?** | Yes — a forest can be replaced by a factory | No — critical ecological functions cannot be replaced | | **Thresholds** | No hard limits; only aggregate capital stock matters | Hard limits (Planetary Boundaries) must not be crossed | | **Practical implication** | Allows deforestation if economic growth compensates | Forbids deforestation beyond regeneration rate | | **Proponent views** | Neo-classical economists (Solow, Hartwick) | Ecological economists (Daly, Costanza) | | **Industrial example** | Carbon offsetting (plant trees elsewhere) | Absolute emission reduction at source | The **Planetary Boundaries framework (Rockström et al., 2009)** strongly favours the *Strong Sustainability* approach by defining **9 quantitative thresholds** that humanity must not transgress. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain in detail the **environmental dimension of sustainability**. Discuss any **four environmental indicators** used to measure manufacturing sustainability, with one example each. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A manufacturing firm produces **10,000 units/month** of a product. Each unit consumes **2.5 kWh** of electricity (grid emission factor **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh**) and uses **0.8 litres** of water. Calculate the **monthly carbon footprint** and **monthly water footprint**. Discuss the mitigation strategies. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A(a): Environmental Dimension** The environmental dimension of sustainability refers to a manufacturing firm's responsibility to operate within the **carrying capacity of the Earth's ecosystems**. It involves: 1. **Resource Conservation** — minimising virgin material and energy use. 2. **Pollution Prevention** — reducing emissions, effluents, and waste. 3. **Ecosystem Protection** — preserving biodiversity, soil, and water bodies. 4. **Circularity** — closing material loops through recycling and remanufacturing. **Four Key Environmental Indicators:** | Indicator | Formula | Manufacturing Example | |---|---|---| | **Carbon Footprint (CF)** | $\sum AD_i \cdot EF_i$ | Steel plant emitting CO₂ from blast furnace | | **Water Footprint (WF)** | $WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | Textile dyeing consuming 200 L water/kg fabric | | **Material Intensity (MI)** | Mass input / Unit output | Auto industry using kg steel per vehicle | | **Waste Recycling Rate (WRR)** | $\frac{\text{Waste Recycled}}{\text{Total Waste}} \times 100$ | Aluminium smelter recycling 95% of scrap | Other indicators include **Eco-Efficiency (GVA / Environmental Impact)**, **Acidification Potential**, and **Eutrophication Potential**. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A(b): Numerical Calculation** **Given:** - Production = 10,000 units/month - Electricity per unit = 2.5 kWh - Grid Emission Factor (EF) = **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh** - Water per unit = 0.8 litres **Step 1 — Total Monthly Electricity Consumption** $$ E_{total} = 10{,}000 \times 2.5 = 25{,}000 \text{ kWh/month} $$ **Step 2 — Monthly Carbon Footprint** $$ CF = E_{total} \times EF = 25{,}000 \times 0.82 = 20{,}500 \text{ kg CO₂e/month} $$ **Step 3 — Monthly Water Footprint (blue component)** $$ WF = 10{,}000 \times 0.8 = 8{,}000 \text{ litres/month} = 8 \text{ m}^3/\text{month} $$ **Step 4 — Mitigation Strategies** 1. **Renewable Energy** — install rooftop solar PV to displace grid electricity. 2. **Energy Efficiency** — replace conventional motors with IE3/IE4 efficiency class. 3. **Water Recycling** — install a **closed-loop water system** to recycle 70%+ of process water. 4. **Process Optimisation** — adopt **lean manufacturing** to eliminate wasteful energy use. 5. **Carbon Offsetting** — purchase **verified carbon credits** as a residual measure. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only **"use renewable energy"** without quantifying. For full marks, you **must show numerical calculation first**, then propose mitigation. Also, do not confuse **Water Footprint (m³)** with **Water Withdrawal (m³)** — they are not identical. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified] **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss in detail the **economic and social dimensions of sustainability** with relevant manufacturing examples. How are they interlinked? **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A company invests **₹ 1 crore** in a worker welfare programme. The present value of measurable social benefits (reduced attrition, lower healthcare costs, productivity gains) is estimated at **₹ 2.4 crore**. Calculate the **SROI** and interpret the result. What additional qualitative benefits should be considered? --- ### **Model Solution — Question B(a): Economic and Social Dimensions** **Economic Dimension** focuses on long-term financial health, encompassing: - **Profitability** — net positive returns over the product life cycle. - **Cost Optimisation** — including avoided externalities. - **Risk Management** — supply-chain resilience, regulatory compliance. - **Value Creation** — for shareholders, employees, and community. *Example:* Toyota's **hybrid Prius** programme was initially loss-making but built long-term brand equity and market leadership in green vehicles — an economically sustainable strategy. **Social Dimension** focuses on human well-being: - **Fair Labour Practices** — minimum wages, no discrimination. - **Health & Safety** — reducing LTIFR. - **Community Development** — local employment, skill training. - **Product Responsibility** — consumer safety, transparency. *Example:* Nestlé's transition to **sustainably sourced cocoa** improves farmer livelihoods (social ✓) while securing long-term raw-material supply (economic ✓). **Interlinkage:** Economic sustainability funds social programmes; healthy communities provide a productive workforce; ethical operations reduce reputational and regulatory risk. The three pillars thus form a **virtuous cycle**. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B(b): SROI Calculation** **Given:** - Investment = ₹ 1,00,00,000 - Present Value of Social Benefits = ₹ 2,40,00,000 **Step 1 — Calculate SROI Ratio** $$ SROI = \frac{PV(\text{Social Benefits})}{PV(\text{Investment})} = \frac{2{,}40{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} $$ $$ SROI = 2.4 : 1 $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation** Every **₹ 1 invested** generates **₹ 2.40** of measurable social value. This is a **strong business case** for scaling the welfare programme. **Step 3 — Qualitative Benefits (Not in SROI)** 1. **Improved brand reputation** and customer loyalty. 2. **Higher employee morale** and innovation. 3. **Better community relations** — reduced social licence-to-operate risk. 4. **Reduced gender-based inequality** through inclusive policies. 5. **Enhanced ESG ratings** attracting ethical investors. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** ignore the **deadweight**, **attribution**, and **drop-off** factors in SROI — KTU examiners specifically test awareness of these. **Deadweight** = benefit that would have happened anyway. **Attribution** = share due to others. **Drop-off** = declining benefit over years. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Brundtland Definition (1987):** Sustainability = meeting present needs without compromising future generations. - **Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994):** People + Planet + Prosperity. - **Three Pillars are mutually dependent** — one weak leg collapses the entire system. - **Environmental indicators:** Carbon Footprint, Water Footprint, Ecological Footprint, Material Intensity, Waste Recycling Rate. - **Economic indicators:** LCC, Material Productivity, External Costs, SROI. - **Social indicators:** LTIFR, Gini Coefficient, SROI, Fair Wage ratios. - **Weak vs Strong Sustainability:** Substitutability of capital is the key debate. - **Planetary Boundaries (Rockström, 2009):** 9 quantitative thresholds — supports Strong Sustainability. - **Always quantify before discussing mitigation** in numerical problems. - **Cite Elkington (1994) and Brundtland (1987)** verbatim when introducing TBL. - **Mind the SROI corrections:** Deadweight, Attribution, Drop-off. - **LCC ≠ Purchase Price** — include OpEx, Maintenance, End-of-Life, Residual Value. - **Use aligned equations with `\vert` or `\mid`, never raw `\vert` inside Markdown tables** (KTU LaTeX protocol). ---

topics.title: Relation Between Green, Lean and Sustainable Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT631 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Relation Between Green, Lean and Sustainable Manufacturing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic, socio-technical methodology primarily derived from the Toyota Production System (TPS) that focuses on the systematic elimination of waste (*Muda*), unevenness (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*) to create maximum value for the end customer using fewer resources, lower capital investment, and reduced lead time. **Green Manufacturing** is an advanced production paradigm that integrates environmental considerations into the product design, manufacturing process, and end-of-life management stages, with the primary objective of reducing the negative ecological footprint—specifically minimizing energy consumption, raw material usage, emissions, effluents, and waste generation at the source itself. **Sustainable Manufacturing** is the holistic, integrated paradigm that creates manufactured products through economically viable processes that minimize negative environmental impacts while conserving energy and natural resources, while simultaneously ensuring safety for employees, customers, and communities, and providing meaningful social value to all stakeholders across the entire product life cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Clarification:** > The three terms are **NOT synonyms** and are **NOT interchangeable** in valuation. Sustainable Manufacturing is the *superset* (umbrella), Green Manufacturing is the *environmental subset*, and Lean Manufacturing is the *efficiency/waste subset*. This hierarchy is a frequently asked 14-mark question. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Analogy) Imagine you are running a **small family restaurant** in Kerala preparing *Appam and Stew*: - **Lean Manufacturing** is like a chef who meticulously organizes the kitchen so that every motion has a purpose. He places the coconut scraper next to the grinder, pre-measures the spices, and throws away no coconut milk. He focuses on **speed, cost, and efficiency**. The kitchen becomes faster, and the customer gets food quickly. However, the chef may still be using non-biodegradable plastic carry bags and wasting leftover stew. - **Green Manufacturing** is when the same chef now switches to **eco-friendly banana leaves** for serving, installs a **biogas plant** for kitchen waste, and uses **solar panels** for the oven. The environmental damage is reduced. However, the chef may still be overproducing stew (overproduction = waste) and inefficiently using gas. - **Sustainable Manufacturing** is the *perfect chef* who combines BOTH: 1. **Lean's** efficiency (no wasted spice, no motion waste, no overproduction) 2. **Green's** environmental care (biogas, banana leaves, solar) 3. **PLUS** societal well-being: pays workers fair wages, sources local tapioca from nearby farmers (social pillar), runs the kitchen profitably (economic pillar). Therefore, **Sustainability = Lean (Efficiency) + Green (Environment) + Social Responsibility + Economic Viability**. > [!NOTE] > **The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Principle (John Elkington, 1994):** > A sustainable manufacturer optimizes three Ps simultaneously: > - **Planet** (Green) > - **People** (Social/Lean respect for humanity) > - **Profit** (Lean economics) ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Quantitative Indicators (in Bold) | Paradigm | Primary Performance Indicator | Standard Notation | |---|---|---| | **Lean** | **Lead Time Reduction, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness), Takt Time** | **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** | | **Green** | **Carbon Footprint, EOL Recovery Rate, Energy per Unit (kWh/unit), Water Footprint** | **CF = Σ(Activity Data × Emission Factor)** | | **Sustainable** | **Triple Bottom Line Score, Life Cycle Sustainability Index** | **LSI = (E + S + En)/3** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Muda, Mura, Muri (Toyota's 3Ms):** Lean manufacturing's foundational principle. *Muda* refers to the **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)**: **T**ransport, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **W**aiting, **O**verproduction, **O**ver-processing, **D**efects. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Three Paradigms #### A. Lean Manufacturing — The *Efficiency* Philosophy - **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS), Japan, post-WWII (Taiichi Ohno). - **Core Focus:** Internal operational excellence; reducing process lead time and **non-value-adding (NVA)** activities. - **Toolkit:** 5S, Value Stream Mapping (VSM), Kanban, Poka-Yoke, Just-In-Time (JIT), Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED), Heijunka. - **Why:** To maximize **customer value** and **profit** while eliminating waste. - **How:** By mapping every process step into Value-Added (VA), Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA), and Pure Waste (NVA). - **What it does NOT focus on:** Environmental emissions directly, social equity of supply chain, product end-of-life. #### B. Green Manufacturing — The *Environmental* Philosophy - **Origin:** Brundtland Report (1987) → Rio Earth Summit (1992) → ISO 14001 (1996). - **Core Focus:** Reducing the **ecological footprint** of industrial operations. - **Toolkit:** Design for Environment (DfE), Design for Disassembly (DfD), Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C), Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), Pollution Prevention (P2), Cleaner Production. - **Why:** To combat climate change, resource depletion, and ecosystem degradation. - **How:** By substituting hazardous materials, redesigning products for recyclability, capturing emissions, and adopting renewable energy. - **What it does NOT focus on:** Waste in motion, process flow efficiency, worker utilization rates. #### C. Sustainable Manufacturing — The *Holistic Integration* Philosophy - **Origin:** Brundtland Commission (1987) + Brundtland Definition of Sustainable Development. - **Core Focus:** The **Triple Bottom Line** — synchronizing economic, environmental, and social performance. - **Toolkit:** Integrated Product Policy (IPP), Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR), Circular Economy models, Industrial Ecology, Sustainable Supply Chain Management (SSCM). - **Why:** To ensure **inter-generational equity** — meeting present needs without compromising the future. - **How:** By aligning Lean efficiency with Green environmentalism and adding the social dimension (fair labor, community welfare). ### 2.2 The Inter-Relational Logic: How They Connect The relationship can be mathematically expressed as a **set-theoretic relationship**: $$\text{Sustainable Manufacturing} = f(\text{Lean Manufacturing} \cup \text{Green Manufacturing} \cup \text{Social Responsibility})$$ **Key Logistical Connections:** 1. **Lean ∩ Green → Resource Efficiency Synergy** Lean's waste elimination directly supports Green's resource conservation. Removing motion waste = less energy; removing defects = less scrap to landfill. 2. **Lean → Green (Indirect Contribution)** Less inventory = less energy for storage; less overproduction = less raw material extraction. 3. **Green → Lean (Enabling Constraint)** Green regulations (emission caps) force process redesign, which often reveals hidden waste, enabling Lean improvements. 4. **Sustainable → (Lean + Green + Social)** The umbrella that demands both, and adds the social equity component (e.g., ISO 26000, SA8000). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Critical) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** This table uses `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value notation to preserve Markdown table syntax. Always use this convention in your answer scripts. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ where $A$=Availability, $P$=Performance, $Q$=Quality | Dimensionless (%) | Lean metric for asset utilization | | 2 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | seconds/unit | Lean pace-setting | | 3 | **Carbon Footprint (CF)** | $\text{CF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AD_i \times EF_i)$ | tCO₂e | Green metric for GHG emissions | | 4 | **Material Efficiency (ME)** | $\text{ME} = \dfrac{M_{output}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ | % | Green material utilization | | 5 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{N_{units produced}}$ | kWh/unit or MJ/unit | Green energy benchmark | | 6 | **Recyclability Index (RI)** | $\text{RI} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} (m_i \times r_i)}{M_{total}}$ | % | Green end-of-life design | | 7 | **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)** | Sum of Transport + Inventory + Motion + Waiting + Overproduction + Over-processing + Defects | — | Lean diagnostic | | 8 | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{T_{value\text{-}added}}{T_{total\ lead\ time}} \times 100\%$ | % | Lean process efficiency | | 9 | **Triple Bottom Line Score (TBL)** | $\text{TBL} = \alpha E + \beta S + \gamma En$ where $E$=Economic, $S$=Social, $En$=Environmental, and $\alpha+\beta+\gamma = 1$ | Normalized score | Sustainability composite index | | 10 | **Productivity** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | units/hour or ₹/hour | Universal efficiency measure | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Sector:** Toyota's adoption of Lean + Green practices (e.g., hybrid Prius) demonstrates the integration — fuel efficiency (Lean economy) + low emissions (Green) + job creation (Social). - **Electronics:** Apple's *Liam* robot disassembles iPhones for recycling — combines Green (DfD) with Lean (automated cell disassembly). - **KTU Industrial Visits Context:** A CNC machine shop applying Lean (5S + SMED) + Green (solar-powered coolant pumps + chip recycling) = Sustainable cell. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: OEE as a Lean Performance Metric **Starting Premise:** OEE is the gold-standard metric for measuring manufacturing productivity in Lean systems, identifying the **six big losses** that occur in any manufacturing process. **Step 1:** Define the three component ratios that constitute OEE. $$\text{Availability} = A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$ **Step 2:** Define the Performance ratio, which captures the speed losses. $$\text{Performance} = P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$ **Step 3:** Define the Quality ratio, which captures the defect losses. $$\text{Quality} = Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces Produced}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply the three ratios to obtain the composite OEE score. $$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$ **Step 5:** World-class benchmark values (industry standard): $$\text{OEE}_{world\text{-}class} \geq 85\%$$ This means $\text{Availability} \geq 90\%$, $\text{Performance} \geq 95\%$, and $\text{Quality} \geq 99\%$. **Conversion Logic:** Each of the three ratios is bounded between 0 and 1, hence OEE itself is a fractional score where 100% represents perfect production (no downtime, no slow cycles, no defects). The product form (not sum) is used because a *bottleneck* in any one dimension degrades the *whole* system — analogous to a series electrical circuit where total resistance is the product of individual resistances normalized. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) — Lean Diagnostic Decomposition Lean philosophy identifies seven primary categories of waste. Each is quantified relative to its cost of occurrence. **Step 1:** The general waste function is: $$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} W_i = W_T + W_I + W_M + W_W + W_{OP} + W_{OP+} + W_D$$ Where: - $W_T$ = **T**ransport waste (unnecessary movement of materials) - $W_I$ = **I**nventory waste (excess raw material, WIP, finished goods) - $W_M$ = **M**otion waste (unnecessary movement of people) - $W_W$ = **W**aiting waste (idle time of people or machines) - $W_{OP}$ = **O**ver**p**roduction waste (producing more than needed) - $W_{OP+}$ = **O**ver-**p**rocessing waste (doing more work than required) - $W_D$ = **D**efects waste (rework, scrap, warranty claims) **Step 2:** Value-Added Ratio (VAR) is the inverse indicator of waste: $$\text{VAR} = \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{VA} + T_{NVA}} \times 100\%$$ Where $T_{VA}$ = Value-Added time and $T_{NVA}$ = Non-Value-Added time. **Conversion Logic:** In Lean, the goal is to **maximize VAR** by reducing $T_{NVA}$. Activities that do not transform the product toward what the customer is willing to pay for are classified as waste and targeted for elimination. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Carbon Footprint Calculation (Green Manufacturing) **Step 1:** The carbon footprint of a manufacturing process is the sum of all greenhouse gas emissions converted to CO₂-equivalent units. $$\text{CF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$$ Where: - $AD_i$ = Activity Data (e.g., liters of diesel, kWh of electricity, tonnes of waste) - $EF_i$ = Emission Factor (e.g., kgCO₂/liter of diesel) - $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential (dimensionless multiplier, CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265) **Step 2:** The unit of CF: $$[\text{CF}] = \text{kgCO}_2\text{e (kilograms of CO}_2\text{ equivalent)}$$ **Step 3:** Reduction in carbon footprint over time: $$\Delta \text{CF} = \frac{\text{CF}_{baseline} - \text{CF}_{new}}{\text{CF}_{baseline}} \times 100\%$$ **Conversion Logic:** Each emission source (Scope 1: direct, Scope 2: indirect from electricity, Scope 3: value chain) is converted to a common unit via the GWP factor, allowing aggregation. This is the *exact* principle used in ISO 14064 and the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Integration Matrix: Lean × Green × Sustainable This is a **high-yield 14-mark analytical table** you may be asked to construct in the exam. | Performance Dimension | Lean Contribution | Green Contribution | Sustainability Net Effect | |---|---|---|---| | **Material Usage** | Reduces excess inventory and over-processing | Promotes recyclable, non-toxic inputs | Optimal material circularity | | **Energy** | Eliminates idle running of machines | Switches to renewables (solar/wind) | Lowest possible energy intensity | | **Waste (Solid)** | Reduces scrap via Poka-Yoke, root cause analysis | Diverts remaining waste via 5R (Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover) | Zero-Landfill aspiration | | **Waste (Emissions)** | Indirect reduction via efficiency | Direct reduction via cleaner tech | Net-zero carbon pathway | | **Worker Safety** | Ergonomic motion design, 5S cleanliness | Reduced chemical exposure, safer alternatives | Healthy workforce, low absenteeism | | **Lead Time** | Compressed via VSM, SMED, Kanban | Often lengthened (slower eco-materials) | Balanced: fast + responsible | | **Cost** | Lowered via waste elimination | Higher initially (green tech CapEx) | Lower Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) | | **Product Life** | Unaddressed directly | Extended via DfD, modular design | Circular product lifespan | | **Stakeholder Trust** | Limited scope (internal) | Moderate (environmental CSR) | Maximum (TBL transparency) | **Synthesis:** The matrix reveals a crucial insight — **Lean and Green are mutually reinforcing in the long run, but they may exhibit short-term trade-offs (e.g., Green may increase cost, Lean may increase throughput at the cost of the environment if pursued alone).** Sustainability reconciles this by mandating the *long-term, life-cycle* view. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Venn Diagram — Set-Theoretic Relationship of the Three Paradigms ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SM["SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
(The Umbrella)"] direction TB subgraph INTERSECTION[" "] LG["LEAN + GREEN
Resource Efficiency Zone"] end LM["LEAN
Mfg"] GM["GREEN
Mfg"] SOC["SOCIAL
Responsibility"] ECO["ECONOMIC
Viability"] end LM -.->|"Waste
Elimination"| LG GM -.->|"Eco
Design"| LG LG ==> SM SOC ==> SM ECO ==> SM style SM fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:3px style LM fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px style GM fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px style LG fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px style SOC fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,stroke-width:2px style ECO fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The *center* of the Venn (Lean ∩ Green) represents the **highest-value zone** where operational efficiency and environmental performance converge. Sustainable Manufacturing is the *encompassing* circle that also includes the Social and Economic dimensions. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Integration Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INPUT STAGE
Raw Materials & Energy"] B["LEAN CELL
VSM and JIT"] C["GREEN CELL
Cleaner Production"] D["SOCIAL CELL
Worker Safety & CSR"] E["ECONOMIC CELL
Cost and Profit"] F["OUTPUT STAGE
Product plus Co-Products"] G["END OF LIFE
Recycle / Remanufacture"] A --> B A --> C B --> D C --> D D --> E B --> F C --> F F --> G G -.->|"Closed Loop"| A style A fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** This represents the **circular flow** of a sustainable manufacturing system. Material inputs pass through Lean (efficiency), Green (clean processes), and Social (human-centric) cells, and the End-of-Life loop **re-feeds** recovered materials back to Input — this is the *Circular Economy* in action. --- ### 4.3 Comparative Evolution Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["1950s
Mass Production
High Waste"] P2["1970s
Lean TPS
Toyota"] P3["1990s
Green Mfg
ISO 14001"] P4["2000s
Lean + Green
Integration"] P5["2010s onwards
Sustainable Mfg
Industry 4.0 + TBL"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 style P1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style P2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style P3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style P4 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298 style P5 fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2d6a4f ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Manufacturing paradigms have *evolved* — from pure mass production (high waste) → Lean (efficiency) → Green (environment) → Lean-Green integration → Holistic Sustainable Manufacturing. This is the most commonly asked **evolutionary question** in KTU exams. --- ### 4.4 Waste Hierarchy (5R / 6R) — Green Manufacturing Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD R0["Material End-of-Life
Decision Point"] R1["1. REFUSE
Do not use at all"] R2["2. REDUCE
Use minimum quantity"] R3["3. REUSE
Use again for same function"] R4["4. RECYCLE
Reprocess into raw material"] R5["5. RECOVERY
Energy recovery incineration"] R6["6. DISPOSAL
Landfill Last Resort"] R0 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> R4 R4 --> R5 R5 --> R6 style R1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style R2 fill:#b6f0b6,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px style R3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px style R4 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px style R5 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style R6 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **6R Waste Hierarchy** mandated by the EU Waste Framework Directive and widely used in Green Manufacturing. The **most preferred option is at the top** (Refuse) and the **least preferred is at the bottom** (Disposal). Students often incorrectly list Disposal first. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Differentiate between Lean Manufacturing and Green Manufacturing. List any three tools used in each paradigm.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 1.5 for differentiation + 1.5 for tools):** | Aspect | Lean Manufacturing | Green Manufacturing | |---|---|---| | **Primary Focus** | Elimination of operational waste (Muda) | Reduction of environmental impact | | **Origin** | Toyota Production System (TPS) | Brundtland Report, ISO 14001 | | **Key Metrics** | Lead time, OEE, Takt time, VAR | Carbon footprint, EI, recyclability | | **Scope** | Internal process efficiency | Product life cycle (cradle to grave) | | **Driving Force** | Customer value + profit | Environmental regulation + ethics | | **3 Tools Each** | 5S, Kanban, VSM, SMED, Poka-Yoke, JIT | LCA, DfE, DfD, Cleaner Production, Cradle-to-Cradle, ISO 14001 | *(Examiner's note: Full marks require a tabular comparison and 3 tools each.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Explain the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework. Why is it central to Sustainable Manufacturing?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework, coined by **John Elkington (1994)**, asserts that a manufacturing firm's performance must be evaluated on **three dimensions simultaneously**: 1. **Economic (Profit):** The firm must be financially viable — covering costs, generating ROI, and ensuring long-term solvency. 2. **Environmental (Planet):** The firm must minimize its ecological footprint — emissions, effluents, resource depletion, and waste. 3. **Social (People):** The firm must contribute to societal welfare — fair labor practices, community engagement, worker safety, and product responsibility. **TBL is central to Sustainable Manufacturing** because it expands the conventional single bottom line (profit) into a *comprehensive* measure of corporate health. A firm that is profitable but pollutes a river is **not sustainable**; a firm that is green but bankrupt is **not sustainable**; only a firm that balances all three pillars is genuinely **sustainable**. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A: 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the relationship between Green, Lean, and Sustainable Manufacturing. Justify why Sustainable Manufacturing is described as a *superset* of the other two. **(b) [7 Marks]** A CNC machining cell operates 8 hours per shift with two shifts per day. The cell experiences 45 minutes of downtime per shift, produces 380 components per shift, of which 12 are rejected, and the ideal cycle time is 28 seconds. Calculate the OEE, VAR, and comment on the cell's performance. --- ### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 [2 marks]:** Define each paradigm with a focus on its specific contribution. - **Lean Manufacturing:** Focuses on **operational efficiency** by eliminating the **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)** — Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects. Originated at Toyota. - **Green Manufacturing:** Focuses on **environmental stewardship** by minimizing energy, materials, emissions, and waste across the product's life cycle. Anchored in ISO 14001 and the Brundtland Report. - **Sustainable Manufacturing:** The **holistic paradigm** that integrates economic viability, environmental protection, and social equity — the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**. **Step 2 [3 marks]:** Draw a labeled Venn diagram (as in Section 4.1) showing: - **Lean (L):** Efficiency subset - **Green (G):** Environment subset - **Sustainable (S):** The encompassing circle (L ∪ G ∪ Social ∪ Economic) **Step 3 [2 marks]:** Justify the "superset" statement. Sustainable Manufacturing is the **superset** because: - It **includes** all of Lean's waste-elimination principles. - It **includes** all of Green's environmental principles. - It **adds** the social responsibility dimension (e.g., fair labor, community welfare, ISO 26000). - It **adds** the long-term economic viability dimension (e.g., life-cycle costing, circular economy). In set notation: $$S \supseteq L \cup G$$ where $S$ = Sustainable, $L$ = Lean, $G$ = Green. Both Lean and Green are **necessary but not sufficient** conditions for sustainability; the social and economic pillars complete the framework. --- ### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given Data:** - Shift time: $T_{shift} = 8$ hours $= 480$ minutes - Shifts per day: 2 - Downtime per shift: $D = 45$ minutes - Total pieces per shift: $N = 380$ - Rejected pieces per shift: $R = 12$ - Ideal cycle time: $CT_{ideal} = 28$ seconds **Step 1 [2 marks]: Calculate Availability (A).** $$A = \frac{T_{shift} - D}{T_{shift}} = \frac{480 - 45}{480} = \frac{435}{480} = 0.90625 = 90.63\%$$ **Step 2 [2 marks]: Calculate Performance (P).** Operating time per shift = $T_{shift} - D = 435$ minutes $= 26{,}100$ seconds. Good + rejected pieces = $380$ per shift. $$P = \frac{CT_{ideal} \times N}{T_{operating}} = \frac{28 \times 380}{26{,}100} = \frac{10{,}640}{26{,}100} = 0.4077 = 40.77\%$$ **Step 3 [1.5 marks]: Calculate Quality (Q).** $$Q = \frac{N - R}{N} = \frac{380 - 12}{380} = \frac{368}{380} = 0.9684 = 96.84\%$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]: Calculate OEE.** $$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9063 \times 0.4077 \times 0.9684$$ $$\text{OEE} = 0.3578 = 35.78\%$$ **Step 5 [0.5 marks]: Comment on performance.** An OEE of **35.78%** is **far below** the world-class benchmark of **≥ 85%**. The cell is critically underperforming, primarily due to **low performance ratio (40.77%)** — meaning the cell is running much slower than its ideal cycle time. Recommended actions: SMED, changeover optimization, and equipment speed calibration. A Takt Time analysis and bottleneck identification are also advised. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) of Lean Manufacturing. For each waste, give one engineering example and one elimination strategy. **(b) [7 Marks]** A foundry consumes 2,400 kWh of electricity, 800 liters of diesel, and generates 1.5 tonnes of metal scrap per month. Given the emission factors: electricity = 0.82 kgCO₂/kWh, diesel = 2.68 kgCO₂/L, and scrap recycling offset = 0.45 kgCO₂/kg, calculate the **net monthly carbon footprint** and suggest **three Green Manufacturing strategies** to reduce it. --- ### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** | # | Waste | Definition | Engineering Example | Elimination Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **T**ransport | Unnecessary movement of materials between processes | Moving raw materials 5 times across a factory | Optimize plant layout (cellular manufacturing) | | 2 | **I**nventory | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods | Storing 6 months of bolts on the shop floor | Implement Just-In-Time (JIT) delivery | | 3 | **M**otion | Unnecessary movement of workers | Operator walking 50 m to fetch a tool | Apply 5S — place tools at point of use | | 4 | **W**aiting | Idle time of machines or operators | CNC machine idle while operator sets up next job | Implement SMED to reduce changeover time | | 5 | **O**ver**p**roduction | Producing more than the customer requires | Printing 1000 brochures when only 600 are ordered | Use Kanban pull system, level production (Heijunka) | | 6 | **O**ver-**p**rocessing | Doing more work than the customer values | Polishing a surface that will be hidden inside assembly | Apply Value Stream Mapping to remove NNVA steps | | 7 | **D**efects | Producing non-conforming parts requiring rework | 5% rejection rate in a welding line | Install Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) devices | *(Each row = 1 mark. Examiner's note: A tabular format is expected; full sentence explanations are required.)* --- ### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given Data:** - Electricity consumption: $E_{elec} = 2{,}400$ kWh/month, $EF_{elec} = 0.82$ kgCO₂/kWh - Diesel consumption: $D_{fuel} = 800$ L/month, $EF_{diesel} = 2.68$ kgCO₂/L - Metal scrap generated: $S = 1{,}5\text{ tonnes} = 1{,}500$ kg/month - Scrap recycling offset: $OF_{scrap} = 0.45$ kgCO₂/kg **Step 1 [2 marks]: Calculate gross emissions from electricity.** $$E_{elec} = 2{,}400 \times 0.82 = 1{,}968 \text{ kgCO}_2$$ **Step 2 [2 marks]: Calculate gross emissions from diesel.** $$E_{diesel} = 800 \times 2.68 = 2{,}144 \text{ kgCO}_2$$ **Step 3 [1.5 marks]: Calculate carbon offset from scrap recycling.** $$E_{offset} = 1{,}500 \times 0.45 = 675 \text{ kgCO}_2$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]: Calculate net carbon footprint.** $$\text{CF}_{net} = E_{elec} + E_{diesel} - E_{offset}$$ $$\text{CF}_{net} = 1{,}968 + 2{,}144 - 675 = 3{,}437 \text{ kgCO}_2/\text{month}$$ $$\text{CF}_{net} = 3.437 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/month} \approx 41.24 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/year}$$ **Step 5 [0.5 marks]: Three Green Manufacturing strategies.** 1. **Renewable Energy Integration:** Install rooftop solar PV to offset grid electricity — target 50% renewable share within 2 years. 2. **Energy-Efficient Equipment:** Replace diesel furnaces with induction or electric alternatives; install VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives) on motors. 3. **Closed-Loop Scrap Recovery:** Establish an in-house remelting and casting line to achieve **zero waste-to-landfill** and double the recycling credit. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Read Carefully):** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "Sustainability" with "Green".** Many students use them interchangeably. Sustainable = TBL; Green = only environment. Valuation key deducts 3 marks for this. > 2. **Do NOT write vague answers** like "Lean is about waste" without naming the 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) and at least 3 specific tools. > 3. **Always present numerical answers with units** (e.g., kgCO₂, %, seconds). Bare numbers without units = –1 mark. > 4. **In OEE calculations, never skip intermediate steps.** A direct jump to the final answer without showing $A$, $P$, $Q$ individually = –2 marks. > 5. **Use LaTeX or clear block equations** in your answer script — illegible handwriting on formulas is a top mark-loser in ESE. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)** - ✅ **Lean = Efficiency = TIMWOOD (7 Wastes)** → Tools: 5S, VSM, JIT, Kanban, SMED, Poka-Yoke. - ✅ **Green = Environment = 6R Hierarchy** → Tools: LCA, DfE, DfD, ISO 14001, Cleaner Production. - ✅ **Sustainable = TBL (Planet + People + Profit)** → Tools: CSR, ISO 26000, Circular Economy, Industrial Ecology. - ✅ **Set Relationship:** Sustainable ⊃ (Lean ∪ Green ∪ Social ∪ Economic). - ✅ **OEE Formula:** $A \times P \times Q$; World-class ≥ 85%. - ✅ **Takt Time:** $T_{available} \div D_{demand}$. - ✅ **Carbon Footprint:** Sum of (Activity Data × Emission Factor × GWP). - ✅ **Evolution:** Mass Production → Lean → Green → Lean-Green → Sustainable (Industry 4.0). - ✅ **Sustainable is the SUPERSET, not the SYNONYM.** - ✅ **Lean and Green are MUTUALLY REINFORCING in the long run** (Lean saves resources → Green benefits; Green forces redesign → Lean uncovers waste). - ✅ **Brundtland Report (1987)** = origin of Sustainable Development definition. - ✅ **John Elkington (1994)** = coined *Triple Bottom Line*. - ✅ **Toyota Production System (TPS)** = origin of Lean. - ✅ **ISO 14001 (1996)** = standard for Green/Environmental Management Systems. - ✅ Always write answers in **tabular form** for comparison-type 7-mark questions — examiners reward structured presentation. - ✅ The **Venn diagram and the 6R hierarchy** are *high-yield* diagrams — practice drawing them by hand before exams.

topics.title: Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT631 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD)**, also referred to as *Green QFD* or *Eco-QFD*, is a systematic, customer-driven product and process development framework that extends the classical **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** methodology by integrating **environmental requirements, regulations, and eco-design parameters** as first-class citizens within the *House of Quality (HOQ)* matrix.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ECQFD is a structured planning and engineering tool used in sustainable manufacturing to translate **Voice of Customer (VOC)** — including both *functional* and *environmental* demands — into **Voice of Engineer / Voice of Process (VOE/VOP)**, ensuring that ecological considerations (energy efficiency, recyclability, toxicity, end-of-life disposal, carbon footprint) are embedded at the conceptual design stage rather than being retrofitted later.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):**
> "ECQFD is a matrix-based product development methodology that systematically incorporates environmental attributes into every phase of QFD, from customer requirement analysis to manufacturing process planning, in order to simultaneously optimize product quality, cost, performance, *and* environmental impact."

Mathematically, ECQFD can be expressed as a multi-attribute decision matrix:

$$
ECQFD = f \big( CR_{functional} \cup CR_{environmental},\ TR_{engineering} \cup TR_{eco} \big)
$$

where:
- $CR_{functional}$ = traditional customer requirements (durability, performance, price)
- $CR_{environmental}$ = green customer requirements (recyclability, low energy, biodegradability)
- $TR_{engineering}$ = traditional technical descriptors
- $TR_{eco}$ = eco-design descriptors (material selection index, disassembly time, $CO_2$ emission factor)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are designing a new **electric toothbrush** for a global brand.

In *traditional QFD*, you would ask: *"What does the customer want?"* — soft bristles, 7-day battery life, attractive color, affordable price. You would then translate these into engineering specs like *bristle stiffness (N/m²)*, *battery capacity (mAh)*, and *housing color (RAL code)*.

In **ECQFD**, you add an entirely new wing to the house: *"What does the planet (and the eco-conscious customer) want?"* — recyclable plastic body, replaceable battery (not glued shut), minimal packaging, low-energy charging, RoHS-compliant electronics, biodegradable brush head, and a take-back program for end-of-life.

The **House of Quality** therefore grows an **environmental wing** — the same matrix that engineers use to balance vibration, torque, and ergonomics now *also* must satisfy recycling, energy, and toxicity targets. ECQFD is the blueprint that makes sure your toothbrush is not only *good* but *green* — and the two are engineered together, not in conflict.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Mnemonic:** *"QFD builds the house. ECQFD adds the solar panels, rainwater harvesting, and composting basement — to the same house — before the foundation is poured."*

### 1.3 Historical Evolution (KTU Expected Background)

| Year | Milestone | Contributor |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| 1972 | Origin of QFD in Japan (Mitsubishi Kobe Shipyard) | Akao, Mizuno |
| 1988 | QFD introduced to Western manufacturing | Sullivan, Hauser |
| 1996 | First formal ECQFD framework proposed | Zhang, Kuo, Lu, Chang |
| 1999 | Integration with Design for Environment (DfE) | Gungor & Gupta |
| 2007 | Extension to Product Service Systems (PSS) | Sakao |
| 2020+ | Digital twins, AI-assisted green QFD | Industry 4.0 research |

**Core Philosophy:** *"Pollution Prevention Pays (PPP)"* — by integrating environmental thinking *upstream*, firms avoid costly *end-of-pipe* treatments later.

### 1.4 Why ECQFD? — The Sustainability Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Points (must appear in any 14-mark answer):**
> 1. **Regulatory Compliance** — REACH, RoHS, WEEE, EOL Vehicle Directive, India's E-Waste Management Rules 2022.
> 2. **Market Differentiation** — 73% of millennials (Nielsen 2018) willing to pay premium for sustainable products.
> 3. **Cost Avoidance** — Eco-design reduces warranty, disposal, and compliance costs.
> 4. **Risk Mitigation** — Avoids future *eco-taxes* and *carbon penalties*.
> 5. **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — People, Planet, Profit alignment.

### 1.5 Relationship with Other Sustainable Manufacturing Tools

ECQFD is a *front-end planning* tool that feeds into downstream sustainable engineering methods:

$$
\text{ECQFD} \longrightarrow \text{DFE (Design for Environment)} \longrightarrow \text{DFA (Disassembly Analysis)} \longrightarrow \text{LCA (Life Cycle Assessment)} \longrightarrow \text{Production}
$$

This is the **front-loading** principle — environmental decisions made at the QFD stage are **10× cheaper to implement** than those made at production stage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The House of Quality (HOQ) — Classical vs. ECQFD Architecture

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is a visual matrix that documents the relationships between customer desires and engineering characteristics. ECQFD extends this matrix by inserting an *environmental layer* in multiple rooms.

### 2.1.1 Standard Rooms of an ECQFD House

| Room # | Name | Function | Classical QFD | ECQFD Addition |
|--------|------|----------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Customer Requirements (WHATS) | VOC translated | Functional only | **+ Environmental (Green) VOC** |
| 2 | Planning Matrix | Importance weights, targets | Sales points, technical benchmarks | **+ Eco-targets ($CO_2$, recyclability %)** |
| 3 | Technical Requirements (HOWS) | Engineering response | Mechanical, electrical, thermal | **+ Eco-design descriptors (DfE)** |
| 4 | Relationship Matrix | Strength of link (●, ○, △) | Functional vs. Technical | **+ Environmental relationship cells** |
| 5 | Roof (Correlation Matrix) | Synergy/Trade-off among HOWs | Engineering trade-offs | **+ Eco vs. eco, eco vs. functional** |
| 6 | Technical Importance Rating | Rank of HOWs | Weighted sum | **+ Eco-weighted sum** |
| 7 | Competitive Assessment | Benchmark vs. competitors | Quality/price | **+ Green benchmarking column** |
| 8 | Technical Benchmarks | Unit, range, target | SI units | **+ Environmental targets (g $CO_2$/unit, % recyclability)** |

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of ECQFD (Examination Critical)

#### 2.2.1 Step 1: Customer Requirement Importance Weighting

For *n* customer requirements (functional + environmental), the absolute importance of the *i*-th requirement is:

$$
w_i = \alpha \cdot w_{i}^{func} + (1-\alpha) \cdot w_{i}^{env}
$$

where:
- $w_i$ = combined importance of customer requirement *i*
- $w_{i}^{func}$ = normalized functional importance ($0 \le w_{i}^{func} \le 1$)
- $w_{i}^{env}$ = normalized environmental importance ($0 \le w_{i}^{env} \le 1$)
- $\alpha \in [0,1]$ = strategic weighting factor chosen by management (typical value $\alpha = 0.5$ to $0.7$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If a question gives $\alpha = 0.6$, interpret it as *60% weight to functional, 40% to environmental*. This is a frequent *Part A (3-mark)* question.

#### 2.2.2 Step 2: Relationship Strength Assignment

The relationship between customer requirement *i* and technical response *j* is given by a discrete scale, typically:

| Symbol | Relationship Strength | Numerical Value $r_{ij}$ |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| ● (Strong) | Strong correlation | **9** |
| ○ (Medium) | Moderate correlation | **3** |
| △ (Weak) | Weak correlation | **1** |
| (Blank) | No relationship | **0** |

A *negative* value (e.g., $-3$ or $-9$) is used in the correlation roof to indicate **trade-offs**.

#### 2.2.3 Step 3: Technical Importance Rating (The Core ECQFD Equation)

The technical importance rating for the *j*-th technical requirement is computed as:

$$
TI_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij} \quad \text{(classical QFD)}
$$

For **ECQFD**, the equation is augmented to include a separate environmental summation:

$$
TI_j^{ECQFD} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n_f} w_i^{func} \cdot r_{ij}^{func}}_{\text{Functional contribution}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n_e} w_i^{env} \cdot r_{ij}^{env}}_{\text{Environmental contribution}}
$$

where $n_f$ = number of functional customer requirements and $n_e$ = number of environmental customer requirements.

**Normalized Technical Importance:**

$$
TI_j^{norm} = \frac{TI_j^{ECQFD}}{\max_{k} \left( TI_k^{ECQFD} \right)} \times 100\%
$$

#### 2.2.4 Step 4: Roof Correlation Matrix

The roof captures the inter-dependency among technical responses. For *m* technical descriptors, the roof is an upper-triangular matrix $C$ of size $m \times m$:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix}
c_{11} & c_{12} & \cdots & c_{1m} \\
0 & c_{22} & \cdots & c_{2m} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & \cdots & c_{mm}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

with $c_{jk} \in \{+9, +3, +1, 0, -1, -3, -9\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Alert (Frequent Exam Scenario):** A positive $c_{jk}$ means improving $TR_j$ **also** improves $TR_k$ (synergy). A negative $c_{jk}$ means improving $TR_j$ **worsens** $TR_k$ (e.g., increasing strength *vs.* increasing recyclability — a classic eco-conflict).

#### 2.2.5 Step 5: Eco-Target Setting (Post-ECQFD Output)

The output of the ECQFD is a prioritized list of technical requirements with assigned eco-targets, e.g.:

- **Material Recyclability:** $\geq 75\%$
- **Energy Consumption:** $\leq 0.5$ **kWh/unit**
- **$CO_2$ Footprint:** $\leq 2.5$ **kg $CO_2$-eq/unit**
- **Disassembly Time:** $\leq 90$ **seconds**
- **Hazardous Substance Content:** $\leq 0.1\%$ by weight (RoHS compliance)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $w_i = \alpha w_{i}^{func} + (1-\alpha) w_{i}^{env}$ | Combined importance weight | ★★★★★ |
