| 2 | $TI_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | Technical Importance (classical) | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $TI_j^{ECQFD} = \sum w_i^{func} r_{ij}^{func} + \sum w_i^{env} r_{ij}^{env}$ | ECQFD technical importance | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $TI_j^{norm} = \dfrac{TI_j}{\max(TI_k)} \times 100\%$ | Normalized importance | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $\text{Eco-Score} = \sum w_i^{env} \cdot s_i$ | Aggregate green score | ★★★ |
| 6 | $C_{jk} \in \{-9,-3,-1,0,+1,+3,+9\}$ | Roof correlation values | ★★★ |
| 7 | $DPP = \dfrac{\text{Design Cost}}{\text{Environmental Benefit}}$ | Design Pollution Prevention index | ★★ |
| 8 | $E_d = \dfrac{E_{recycle} + E_{reuse}}{E_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Disassembly/Recyclability efficiency | ★★ |
| 9 | $CO_2^{total} = \sum_{k} m_k \cdot EF_k$ | Product carbon footprint | ★★★ |
| 10 | $LCA_{score} = \sum_{phase} I_{phase} \cdot W_{phase}$ | Life Cycle Assessment aggregate | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Safety Note:** All vertical bars `|x|` in math are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the KTU markdown table above. Examiners also accept `abs(x)`.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of ECQFD

| Industry | ECQFD Application | Reported Benefit |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Automotive (Toyota, BMW) | Eco-design of dashboards, end-of-life recovery | 15-20% weight reduction, 30% recyclability gain |
| Electronics (Apple, Dell) | Take-back program design, RoHS compliance | Eliminated 6 of 6 banned substances |
| Packaging (Unilever, P&G) | Biodegradable containers, minimalist packaging | 40% material reduction, 25% $CO_2$ reduction |
| Appliances (Whirlpool, LG) | Energy Star targets via QFD | 35% energy reduction per unit |
| Construction (Skanska) | Green concrete, low-VOC paints | 50% $CO_2$ reduction in concrete mix |

### 2.5 Conceptual Differences: QFD vs. ECQFD vs. DFE

| Aspect | QFD | ECQFD | DfE |
|--------|-----|-------|-----|
| Focus | Customer satisfaction | Customer + planet | Environment only |
| Stage | Concept-to-process | Concept-to-process with eco overlay | Detailed design |
| Tool | House of Quality | Extended HOQ with eco wing | Eco-checklist, MET matrix |
| Output | Technical specs | Technical + eco specs | Eco-design guidelines |
| Scale | 1-100 importance | 1-100 + 1-100 eco | Pass/Fail eco criteria |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: ECQFD Technical Importance Calculation (14-Mark Type)

**Problem Statement (KTU 2019/2020 Pattern):**

A company is developing a **rechargeable electric kettle** using ECQFD. Two functional customer requirements and two environmental customer requirements are identified, with the following data:

**Customer Requirements (CR) and Importance Weights:**

| CR # | Requirement | Type | Importance $w_i$ (out of 5) |
|------|-------------|------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Fast boiling (5 min) | Functional | 4 |
| 2 | Lightweight (≤ 1.0 kg) | Functional | 3 |
| 3 | Recyclable body (≥ 80%) | Environmental | 5 |
| 4 | Low standby power (≤ 0.5 W) | Environmental | 4 |

**Technical Requirements (TR):**
- $TR_1$ = Heating element power (W)
- $TR_2$ = Body material density (g/cm³)
- $TR_3$ = Polymer recyclability index (%)
- $TR_4$ = Standby circuit power (W)

**Relationship Matrix $r_{ij}$:**

| | $TR_1$ | $TR_2$ | $TR_3$ | $TR_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $CR_1$ (Fast boiling) | ● (9) | ○ (3) | — (0) | △ (1) |
| $CR_2$ (Lightweight) | — (0) | ● (9) | ○ (3) | — (0) |
| $CR_3$ (Recyclable) | — (0) | △ (1) | ● (9) | — (0) |
| $CR_4$ (Low standby) | ○ (3) | — (0) | — (0) | ● (9) |

**Required:** Compute (a) normalized importance weights, (b) raw technical importance for each $TR_j$, (c) normalized technical importance $TI_j^{norm}$, and (d) rank the technical requirements for ECQFD-driven development priority.

### Solution:

#### Step 1: Compute the Sum of Raw Importance Weights

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 4 + 3 + 5 + 4 = 16
$$

#### Step 2: Normalize the Importance Weights

For each $CR_i$:

$$
w_i^{norm} = \frac{w_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k}
$$

Applying the formula:

$$
w_1^{norm} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.2500
$$

$$
w_2^{norm} = \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875
$$

$$
w_3^{norm} = \frac{5}{16} = 0.3125
$$

$$
w_4^{norm} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.2500
$$

**Verification (must sum to 1):**

$$
0.2500 + 0.1875 + 0.3125 + 0.2500 = 1.0000 \checkmark
$$

#### Step 3: Compute the Raw Technical Importance $TI_j$

For each technical requirement, we compute:

$$
TI_j = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i^{norm} \cdot r_{ij}
$$

This is the classical QFD equation, applied to ECQFD since all weights are pre-combined.

**For $TR_1$ (Heating element power):**

$$
TI_1 = (0.2500)(9) + (0.1875)(0) + (0.3125)(0) + (0.2500)(3)
$$

$$
TI_1 = 2.2500 + 0 + 0 + 0.7500
$$

$$
TI_1 = 3.0000
$$

**For $TR_2$ (Body material density):**

$$
TI_2 = (0.2500)(3) + (0.1875)(9) + (0.3125)(1) + (0.2500)(0)
$$

$$
TI_2 = 0.7500 + 1.6875 + 0.3125 + 0
$$

$$
TI_2 = 2.7500
$$

**For $TR_3$ (Polymer recyclability index):**

$$
TI_3 = (0.2500)(0) + (0.1875)(3) + (0.3125)(9) + (0.2500)(0)
$$

$$
TI_3 = 0 + 0.5625 + 2.8125 + 0
$$

$$
TI_3 = 3.3750
$$

**For $TR_4$ (Standby circuit power):**

$$
TI_4 = (0.2500)(1) + (0.1875)(0) + (0.3125)(0) + (0.2500)(9)
$$

$$
TI_4 = 0.2500 + 0 + 0 + 2.2500
$$

$$
TI_4 = 2.5000
$$

#### Step 4: Compute Normalized Technical Importance

Find the maximum $TI_j$:

$$
\max(TI_j) = \max(3.0000,\ 2.7500,\ 3.3750,\ 2.5000) = 3.3750 \quad (TR_3)
$$

Apply the normalization formula:

$$
TI_j^{norm} = \frac{TI_j}{TI_{max}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
TI_1^{norm} = \frac{3.0000}{3.3750} \times 100\% = 88.89\%
$$

$$
TI_2^{norm} = \frac{2.7500}{3.3750} \times 100\% = 81.48\%
$$

$$
TI_3^{norm} = \frac{3.3750}{3.3750} \times 100\% = 100.00\%
$$

$$
TI_4^{norm} = \frac{2.5000}{3.3750} \times 100\% = 74.07\%
$$

#### Step 5: Rank the Technical Requirements

| Rank | $TR$ | $TI_j^{norm}$ | Engineering Priority |
|------|------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $TR_3$ (Polymer recyclability) | 100.00% | **Highest — invest R&D here first** |
| 2 | $TR_1$ (Heating power) | 88.89% | High |
| 3 | $TR_2$ (Body density) | 81.48% | Medium |
| 4 | $TR_4$ (Standby power) | 74.07% | Lower |

**Engineering Interpretation:**

Since the environmental requirement $CR_3$ (Recyclable body) has the highest weight (5/5) and the strongest relationship (● = 9) with $TR_3$, it dominates the technical importance ranking. This tells the engineering team that **selecting a recyclable polymer** is the single most important design decision — *more important than even the heating element power*! This is a typical *eco-shift* that ECQFD reveals but classical QFD would have missed.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Functional vs. Environmental Decomposition (7-Mark Sub-Part Type)

**Problem:** Using the same data above, separate the technical importance into *functional contribution* and *environmental contribution* using the ECQFD augmented formula:

$$
TI_j^{ECQFD} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_f} w_i^{func} r_{ij}^{func} + \sum_{i=1}^{n_e} w_i^{env} r_{ij}^{env}
$$

where $n_f = 2$ (functional CRs), $n_e = 2$ (environmental CRs).

#### Solution:

**Functional weights (normalized within functional set):**

$$
\sum w_i^{func} = 4 + 3 = 7
$$

$$
w_1^{func-norm} = \frac{4}{7} = 0.5714, \quad w_2^{func-norm} = \frac{3}{7} = 0.4286
$$

**Environmental weights (normalized within environmental set):**

$$
\sum w_i^{env} = 5 + 4 = 9
$$

$$
w_3^{env-norm} = \frac{5}{9} = 0.5556, \quad w_4^{env-norm} = \frac{4}{9} = 0.4444
$$

**Functional Contribution $TI_j^{func}$:**

$$
TI_1^{func} = (0.5714)(9) + (0.4286)(0) = 5.1426
$$

$$
TI_2^{func} = (0.5714)(3) + (0.4286)(9) = 1.7142 + 3.8574 = 5.5716
$$

$$
TI_3^{func} = (0.5714)(0) + (0.4286)(3) = 1.2858
$$

$$
TI_4^{func} = (0.5714)(1) + (0.4286)(0) = 0.5714
$$

**Environmental Contribution $TI_j^{env}$:**

$$
TI_1^{env} = (0.5556)(0) + (0.4444)(3) = 1.3332
$$

$$
TI_2^{env} = (0.5556)(1) + (0.4444)(0) = 0.5556
$$

$$
TI_3^{env} = (0.5556)(9) + (0.4444)(0) = 5.0004
$$

$$
TI_4^{env} = (0.5556)(0) + (0.4444)(9) = 4.0000
$$

**Combined ECQFD Importance $TI_j^{ECQFD}$:**

$$
TI_1 = 5.1426 + 1.3332 = 6.4758
$$

$$
TI_2 = 5.5716 + 0.5556 = 6.1272
$$

$$
TI_3 = 1.2858 + 5.0004 = 6.2862
$$

$$
TI_4 = 0.5714 + 4.0000 = 4.5714
$$

**Normalized ECQFD Importance:**

$$
TI_{max} = 6.4758 \quad (TR_1)
$$

$$
TI_1^{norm} = 100.00\%, \quad TI_2^{norm} = 94.61\%, \quad TI_3^{norm} = 97.07\%, \quad TI_4^{norm} = 70.59\%
$$

**Insight:** Notice how the ranking *changes* when you separate functional and environmental contributions. The combined ECQFD view rebalances engineering effort across all four technical areas, preventing the *functional bias* of classical QFD. This is the **fundamental value-add of ECQFD** — a *guaranteed* 7-mark question in any KTU Module 2 examination.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ECQFD Output Decision Tree

$$
\text{ECQFD Output} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{If } TI_j^{env} \gg TI_j^{func}: & \text{Engage DfE specialist first} \\
\text{If } TI_j^{env} \approx TI_j^{func}: & \text{Parallel eco + functional design team} \\
\text{If } TI_j^{env} \ll TI_j^{func}: & \text{Classical QFD sufficient, but verify regulations} \\
\text{If } C_{jk} < 0: & \text{Trigger multi-objective optimization} \\
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: From ECQFD to LCA Linkage

The ECQFD priorities feed directly into **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** phase selection:

$$
\text{Top 3 } TI_j^{ECQFD} \longrightarrow \text{LCA Hotspot Analysis} \longrightarrow \text{Material Substitution} \longrightarrow \text{Process Optimization}
$$

This closed-loop is the foundation of **Sustainable Product Development (SPD)**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

**Q:** Define *Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD)*. List any two key differences between traditional QFD and ECQFD.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD) is a systematic, matrix-based product development framework that extends the classical Quality Function Deployment methodology by incorporating environmental requirements, eco-design parameters, and sustainability targets into the House of Quality, ensuring that ecological considerations are addressed concurrently with functional and cost requirements from the conceptual design stage.

**Two Key Differences (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Traditional QFD | ECQFD |
|--------|-----------------|-------|
| Customer Requirements | Functional only | Functional + Environmental (green VOC) |
| Technical Responses | Engineering only | Engineering + Eco-design descriptors |
| Output Targets | Cost, quality, performance | Cost, quality, performance + $CO_2$, recyclability, toxicity, energy |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — Formula-Based
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]**

**Q:** Write the mathematical expression for *Technical Importance (TI)* in classical QFD and in ECQFD. Explain the additional term in ECQFD.

**Model Answer:**

**Classical QFD (1.5 marks):**

$$
TI_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}
$$

where $w_i$ = importance of customer requirement $i$ and $r_{ij}$ = relationship strength between $CR_i$ and $TR_j$.

**ECQFD (1.5 marks):**

$$
TI_j^{ECQFD} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_f} w_i^{func} r_{ij}^{func} + \sum_{i=1}^{n_e} w_i^{env} r_{ij}^{env}
$$

**Explanation of Additional Term:**
The second summation term in ECQFD explicitly accounts for environmental customer requirements ($CR_{env}$), such as recyclability, low energy consumption, and biodegradability, mapped against eco-design technical responses ($TR_{env}$). This ensures that *green attributes* receive weighted engineering priority instead of being secondary considerations, as in classical QFD.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full ECQFD Computation

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** A consumer electronics company is developing a new **cordless vacuum cleaner** using ECQFD methodology.

The following customer requirements (CRs) and their raw importance weights (on a 1–5 scale) are identified:

| CR # | Customer Requirement | Type | Weight |
|------|----------------------|------|--------|
| 1 | Strong suction (≥ 150 W air-watt) | Functional | 5 |
| 2 | Light weight (≤ 2.0 kg) | Functional | 3 |
| 3 | Rechargeable battery (≥ 30 min runtime) | Functional | 4 |
| 4 | Recyclable plastic body (≥ 70%) | Environmental | 4 |
| 5 | Low noise (≤ 70 dB) | Functional | 3 |
| 6 | Energy Star certified (≤ 200 Wh/charge) | Environmental | 5 |

The technical responses (TRs) and their relationship matrix with CRs are given:

| | $TR_1$ Motor Power (W) | $TR_2$ Body Density (g/cm³) | $TR_3$ Battery Capacity (Ah) | $TR_4$ Polymer Recyclability (%) | $TR_5$ Sound Insulation (dB damp) | $TR_6$ Charging Circuit (W) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $CR_1$ | ● (9) | △ (1) | ○ (3) | — (0) | — (0) | ○ (3) |
| $CR_2$ | — (0) | ● (9) | △ (1) | ○ (3) | — (0) | — (0) |
| $CR_3$ | △ (1) | — (0) | ● (9) | — (0) | — (0) | △ (1) |
| $CR_4$ | — (0) | ○ (3) | — (0) | ● (9) | — (0) | — (0) |
| $CR_5$ | ○ (3) | — (0) | — (0) | — (0) | ● (9) | — (0) |
| $CR_6$ | ○ (3) | — (0) | — (0) | — (0) | — (0) | ● (9) |

**Tasks (Attempt all sub-parts):**

**(a)** Compute the *normalized* customer importance weights for all six CRs. *(4 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the *raw technical importance* $TI_j$ for all six TRs using the ECQFD equation. *(6 marks)*

**(c)** Normalize the technical importance values to 100% scale and rank the technical responses in descending priority. State which $TR$ should receive the highest R&D investment and justify why. *(4 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### (a) Normalized Customer Importance Weights (4 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the sum of all raw weights:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 5 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 5 = 24
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the normalization formula $w_i^{norm} = w_i / \sum w_k$:

$$
w_1^{norm} = \frac{5}{24} = 0.2083
$$

$$
w_2^{norm} = \frac{3}{24} = 0.1250
$$

$$
w_3^{norm} = \frac{4}{24} = 0.1667
$$

$$
w_4^{norm} = \frac{4}{24} = 0.1667
$$

$$
w_5^{norm} = \frac{3}{24} = 0.1250
$$

$$
w_6^{norm} = \frac{5}{24} = 0.2083
$$

**Verification (must equal 1):**

$$
0.2083 + 0.1250 + 0.1667 + 0.1667 + 0.1250 + 0.2083 = 1.0000 \checkmark
$$

---

#### (b) Raw Technical Importance $TI_j$ (6 marks)

**For each $TR_j$, compute $TI_j = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i^{norm} \cdot r_{ij}$:**

**$TI_1$ (Motor Power):**

$$
TI_1 = (0.2083)(9) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.1667)(1) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1250)(3) + (0.2083)(3)
$$

$$
TI_1 = 1.8747 + 0 + 0.1667 + 0 + 0.3750 + 0.6249
$$

$$
\boxed{TI_1 = 3.0413}
$$

**$TI_2$ (Body Density):**

$$
TI_2 = (0.2083)(1) + (0.1250)(9) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1667)(3) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.2083)(0)
$$

$$
TI_2 = 0.2083 + 1.1250 + 0 + 0.5001 + 0 + 0
$$

$$
\boxed{TI_2 = 1.8334}
$$

**$TI_3$ (Battery Capacity):**

$$
TI_3 = (0.2083)(3) + (0.1250)(1) + (0.1667)(9) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.2083)(1)
$$

$$
TI_3 = 0.6249 + 0.1250 + 1.5003 + 0 + 0 + 0.2083
$$

$$
\boxed{TI_3 = 2.4585}
$$

**$TI_4$ (Polymer Recyclability):**

$$
TI_4 = (0.2083)(0) + (0.1250)(3) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1667)(9) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.2083)(0)
$$

$$
TI_4 = 0 + 0.3750 + 0 + 1.5003 + 0 + 0
$$

$$
\boxed{TI_4 = 1.8753}
$$

**$TI_5$ (Sound Insulation):**

$$
TI_5 = (0.2083)(0) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1250)(9) + (0.2083)(0)
$$

$$
TI_5 = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1.1250 + 0
$$

$$
\boxed{TI_5 = 1.1250}
$$

**$TI_6$ (Charging Circuit):**

$$
TI_6 = (0.2083)(3) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.1667)(1) + (0.1667)(0) + (0.1250)(0) + (0.2083)(9)
$$

$$
TI_6 = 0.6249 + 0 + 0.1667 + 0 + 0 + 1.8747
$$

$$
\boxed{TI_6 = 2.6663}
$$

---

#### (c) Normalization and Ranking (4 marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the maximum:

$$
TI_{max} = \max(3.0413,\ 1.8334,\ 2.4585,\ 1.8753,\ 1.1250,\ 2.6663) = 3.0413 \quad (TR_1)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute normalized technical importance $TI_j^{norm} = (TI_j / TI_{max}) \times 100\%$:

$$
TI_1^{norm} = \frac{3.0413}{3.0413} \times 100\% = 100.00\%
$$

$$
TI_2^{norm} = \frac{1.8334}{3.0413} \times 100\% = 60.29\%
$$

$$
TI_3^{norm} = \frac{2.4585}{3.0413} \times 100\% = 80.84\%
$$

$$
TI_4^{norm} = \frac{1.8753}{3.0413} \times 100\% = 61.66\%
$$

$$
TI_5^{norm} = \frac{1.1250}{3.0413} \times 100\% = 36.99\%
$$

$$
TI_6^{norm} = \frac{2.6663}{3.0413} \times 100\% = 87.67\%
$$

**Step 3:** Rank the technical requirements:

| Rank | $TR$ | $TI_j^{norm}$ | Description |
|------|------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $TR_1$ | 100.00% | **Motor Power** — Highest R&D priority |
| 2 | $TR_6$ | 87.67% | Charging Circuit |
| 3 | $TR_3$ | 80.84% | Battery Capacity |
| 4 | $TR_4$ | 61.66% | Polymer Recyclability |
| 5 | $TR_2$ | 60.29% | Body Density |
| 6 | $TR_5$ | 36.99% | Sound Insulation |

**Step 4: Engineering Justification:**

$TR_1$ (Motor Power) receives the highest priority because it has *strong* (● = 9) relationships with the two highest-weighted customer requirements — $CR_1$ (Suction, $w = 5$, $w^{norm} = 0.2083$) and *medium* (○ = 3) relationships with the environmental requirement $CR_6$ (Energy Star, $w = 5$, $w^{norm} = 0.2083$) and $CR_5$ (Low noise). This *cross-functional + cross-environmental* dependency means optimizing the motor design will simultaneously satisfy the strongest customer desires on both functional and environmental wings of the House of Quality.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Conceptual + Partial Computation)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme - Alternative]**

**Q:** 

**(a)** Explain the *architecture* of the House of Quality (HOQ) in ECQFD with a labeled block diagram. List and describe the functions of any *six rooms* of the ECQFD HOQ. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the *role* of ECQFD in sustainable manufacturing. Explain with two real-world industrial examples (automotive and electronics) how ECQFD has been used to integrate environmental considerations into product design. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### (a) ECQFD HOQ Architecture (7 marks)

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** in ECQFD is a visual, matrix-based planning tool composed of interconnected "rooms," each serving a distinct analytical function. The following is the standard ECQFD HOQ architecture:

| Room # | Name | Function | ECQFD Specificity |
|--------|------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Customer Requirements (WHATS)** | Lists all customer needs, both functional and environmental | Includes green VOC (recyclability, low energy, biodegradability) |
| 2 | **Planning Matrix** | Displays importance weights, target values, and competitive benchmarks | Adds eco-targets (e.g., $CO_2$ ≤ X kg, recyclability ≥ Y%) |
| 3 | **Technical Requirements (HOWS)** | Lists engineering responses to the customer requirements | Adds eco-design descriptors (DfE parameters) |
| 4 | **Relationship Matrix (Central)** | Shows strength of correlation between WHATS and HOWS using symbols ●, ○, △ | Environmental relationships explicitly mapped with same scale (9, 3, 1, 0) |
| 5 | **Roof (Correlation Matrix)** | Shows synergy (+) or trade-off (−) between technical requirements | Trade-offs between eco-design parameters (e.g., strength vs. recyclability) |
| 6 | **Technical Importance Ratings** | Output column showing weighted importance of each HOWS | Combines functional + environmental weighted sums; normalized to 100% |

**Block Diagram (descriptive text for exam):**

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
| ROOF (Correlation Matrix: synergy + / trade-off -)       |
+----------+---------------------------+-------------------+
|          |                           |                   |
|  WHATS   |   RELATIONSHIP MATRIX     |  TECH. IMPORTANCE |
| (Func +  |  (CRs vs. TRs: ●○△)       |  RATINGS          |
|  Env)    |                           |  (Output column)  |
|          |                           |                   |
+----------+---------------------------+-------------------+
| PLANNING MATRIX (Importance weights + Eco targets)        |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
| TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS (HOWS): Engineering + Eco-design   |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The left wall = *what the customer (and planet) wants*
- The ceiling/roof = *engineering trade-offs* (e.g., higher motor power ↔ more noise, more energy)
- The floor = *engineering responses* (e.g., motor wattage, polymer grade)
- The output = *prioritized R&D investment list*

---

#### (b) Role of ECQFD in Sustainable Manufacturing + Real-World Examples (7 marks)

**Role of ECQFD in Sustainable Manufacturing (3.5 marks):**

ECQFD plays a **front-end strategic** role in sustainable manufacturing by:

1. **Translating Voice of Customer (VOC) + Voice of Planet (VOP)** into measurable engineering specifications.
2. **Embedding environmental design constraints** at the conceptual stage, where the cost of change is lowest (10× cheaper than retrofitting).
3. **Aligning product development** with international standards (ISO 14040 LCA, ISO 14062 Eco-design, REACH, RoHS).
4. **Driving cross-functional collaboration** between design, manufacturing, and environmental teams.
5. **Quantifying trade-offs** between functional performance and environmental impact (e.g., weight reduction vs. structural strength).
6. **Supporting the Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — People, Planet, Profit.
7. **Facilitating regulatory compliance** before market launch, avoiding penalties and recalls.

**Real-World Example 1: Automotive — Toyota Prius / BMW i-Series (2 marks):**

Toyota used an ECQFD-equivalent process during Prius development to prioritize:
- Lightweight body (high $TI$ for aluminum/composite usage)
- Hybrid powertrain efficiency (high $TI$ for low-emission engine)
- Recyclable interior plastics (high $TI$ for polymer selection)
- End-of-life battery take-back program

The result: ~30% improvement in fuel economy and 95% recyclability of major components, meeting the EU End-of-Life Vehicle Directive 2000/53/EC.

**Real-World Example 2: Electronics — Apple MacBook / Dell Latitude (1.5 marks):**

Apple and Dell integrated ECQFD principles to design the MacBook and Latitude laptop lines with:
- Recyclable aluminum unibody (replacing glued plastic)
- Arsenic-free display glass (RoHS compliance)
- Energy Star 5.0 power supply (≤ 0.5 W standby)
- Modular, screw-based disassembly (replaceable battery, RAM, SSD)

ECQFD helped prioritize these eco-attributes alongside traditional requirements (speed, weight, price), resulting in EPEAT Gold certification and 40% reduction in product carbon footprint across the laptop's lifecycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> **Pitfall 1 — Forgetting to Normalize Weights (–2 marks):** When computing $TI_j$, you *must* first normalize the customer importance weights so that $\sum w_i^{norm} = 1$. If you use raw weights (e.g., 5, 3, 4, 4), the $TI_j$ values will not be on a comparable 0–5 scale and your ranking will be mathematically wrong.
> 
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing the Roof with the Relationship Matrix (–1 mark):** The **roof** is the *correlation* matrix (synergy/trade-off) between technical responses. The **central room** is the *relationship* matrix between CRs and TRs. Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged in a diagram.
> 
> **Pitfall 3 — Missing the Environmental Wing in the Diagram (–2 marks):** A common error in HOQ diagrams is showing only classical CRs. You *must* show a *separate environmental sub-section* in the WHATS room (e.g., shaded green, or labeled "Eco") to demonstrate ECQFD understanding.
> 
> **Pitfall 4 — Not Stating Units for Eco-Targets (–1 mark):** Always mention units: $CO_2$ in **kg $CO_2$-eq**, recyclability in **% by mass**, energy in **kWh/cycle**, toxicity in **ppm or % by weight**.
> 
> **Pitfall 5 — Omitting Trade-off Explanation (–2 marks):** When the roof matrix has a negative entry (e.g., –9), state explicitly *what trades off against what*. Example: "Increasing $TR_1$ (motor power) *worsens* $TR_5$ (noise) — a $\text{-}9$ trade-off."
> 
> **Pitfall 6 — Skipping the Real-World Example (–1 to 2 marks):** A 14-mark answer without *at least one industrial case study* (Toyota, Apple, Philips, Unilever, etc.) loses marks in the "application/real-world" component of the KTU 2024 CO mapping.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — ECQFD):**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [x] ECQFD = *QFD + Environmental Wing* in the House of Quality
- [x] Originator: Zhang, Kuo, Lu, Chang (1996) — *environmentally conscious design*
- [x] Also called *Green QFD* or *Eco-QFD*
- [x] Integrates *Voice of Planet* alongside *Voice of Customer*

### Key Formulae (Must Memorize)
- [x] $w_i = \alpha w_i^{func} + (1 - \alpha) w_i^{env}$
- [x] $TI_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ (classical)
- [x] $TI_j^{ECQFD} = \sum w_i^{func} r_{ij}^{func} + \sum w_i^{env} r_{ij}^{env}$
- [x] $TI_j^{norm} = (TI_j / TI_{max}) \times 100\%$

### HOQ Architecture (Must Draw)
- [x] 6–8 rooms: WHATS, HOWS, Relationship Matrix, Roof, Importance, Benchmarks
- [x] Roof contains synergy (+) and trade-off (−) symbols
- [x] Relationship symbols: ● = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1, blank = 0

### Eco-Design Targets (Must State Units)
- [x] $CO_2$ footprint: **kg $CO_2$-eq/unit**
- [x] Recyclability: **% by mass**
- [x] Energy: **kWh/cycle** or **kWh/1000 hr**
- [x] Disassembly time: **seconds**
- [x] Hazardous substance: **% by weight** (RoHS ≤ 0.1%)

### Standards & Regulations (Must Quote)
- [x] ISO 14040 (LCA), ISO 14062 (Eco-design)
- [x] REACH (EU), RoHS (EU), WEEE (EU)
- [x] EPEAT (US), Energy Star (US)
- [x] E-Waste Management Rules 2022 (India)

### Differences QFD vs. ECQFD (Must Tabulate)
- [x] Classical QFD: only customer function
- [x] ECQFD: customer function + environmental concern
- [x] Classical output: technical specs only
- [x] ECQFD output: technical + eco-targets

### Real-World Industries (Must Cite At Least One)
- [x] Automotive (Toyota Prius, BMW i-Series)
- [x] Electronics (Apple MacBook, Dell Latitude)
- [x] Packaging (Unilever, P&G)
- [x] Appliances (Whirlpool, LG)
- [x] Construction (Skanska)

### Benefits of ECQFD (Must List 3+)
- [x] Upstream eco-decisions (10× cheaper)
- [x] Regulatory compliance
- [x] Market differentiation
- [x] Triple Bottom Line alignment
- [x] Cross-functional team alignment

### Limitations of ECQFD (Know for *Part A*)
- [x] Subjective weighting of $w_i$ values
- [x] Time-consuming matrix construction
- [x] Requires multidisciplinary expertise
- [x] Roof matrix can become complex for large products

### Connection to Other Module 2 Tools
- [x] ECQFD → DfE → DfA → LCA → Production
- [x] ECQFD is the *front-end*, LCA is the *back-end* verification

### KTU 2024 CO Mapping (Likely)
- [x] **CO 2.1:** Explain ECQFD concepts and House of Quality architecture.
- [x] **CO 2.2:** Compute technical importance using ECQFD equations.
- [x] **CO 2.3:** Apply ECQFD to real industrial case studies.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Day Tip:**
> When answering a 14-mark ECQFD question, always structure your answer in this order:
> 1. **Definition** (2 marks)
> 2. **HOQ Architecture** with labeled diagram (3 marks)
> 3. **Mathematical Formulation** (3 marks)
> 4. **Step-by-Step Computation** (3 marks)
> 5. **Engineering Interpretation + Real-World Example** (2 marks)
> 6. **Conclusion** (1 mark)
> 
> This six-part structure aligns with KTU 2024 Scheme OBE assessment rubrics and ensures full marks coverage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R3 and R6 Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT631 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** R3 and R6 Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — The 3R Cycle (R3)

The **R3 Cycle** is the foundational waste hierarchy framework of circular manufacturing, comprising three priority actions in strict descending order of preference:

$$\text{R3 Cycle} = \text{Reduce} \rightarrow \text{Reuse} \rightarrow \text{Recycle}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The 3R principle (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) is a waste management hierarchy that prioritizes minimizing material consumption, extending product lifespans through direct reuse, and finally reprocessing waste streams into secondary raw materials — forming the operational backbone of sustainable manufacturing."*

| R | Action | Operational Meaning |
|---|--------|---------------------|
| **R1** | **Reduce** | Minimize resource input & waste generation at source |
| **R2** | **Reuse** | Use a product/component again for the **same** or **similar** function |
| **R3** | **Recycle** | Reprocess waste into **new raw materials** of equal or lower grade |

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition — The 6R Cycle (R6)

The **R6 Cycle** extends the 3R framework with three additional upstream strategic actions, creating a comprehensive closed-loop manufacturing philosophy:

$$\text{R6 Cycle} = \text{Refuse} \rightarrow \text{Rethink} \rightarrow \text{Reduce} \rightarrow \text{Reuse} \rightarrow \text{Repair} \rightarrow \text{Recycle}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Shift from R3 to R6:**
> R3 is **operational/tactical** (dealing with material that already exists in the system), whereas R6 is **strategic/systemic** (preventing waste at the design and conception stage itself).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Waste Story

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Lunchbox Lifecycle 🍱**
>
> Imagine a KTU student's daily lunchbox:
>
> - **REFUSE** → Say "No" to a plastic bag at the shop. *(Refuse unnecessary packaging)*
> - **RETHINK** → Buy food in bulk once a week instead of daily. *(Rethink consumption patterns)*
> - **REDUCE** → Cook only the quantity you can finish. *(Reduce portion size)*
> - **REUSE** → Wash the steel tiffin box & use it 1000 times. *(Reuse the same box)*
> - **REPAIR** → Get the broken latch fixed by a local shop. *(Repair the broken part)*
> - **RECYCLE** → When the box rusts completely, send the steel to a scrap dealer to be melted into new steel sheets.
>
> **Insight:** The earlier you act in this chain, the **less energy & cost** you expend. Recycling (the last step) still consumes energy; Refusing (the first step) consumes **zero**.

---

## 1.4 Physical & Sustainability Constants Referenced

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — Standard Industry Metrics:**
> - **Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)** — measured on a scale of **0 to 1** (developed by Ellen MacArthur Foundation / Granta Design).
> - **Recycling Rate (RR)** — dimensionless ratio expressed as **%**.
> - **Landfill Diversion Rate** — dimensionless ratio expressed as **%**.
> - **Virgin Material Savings** — measured in **tonnes** or **kg**.
> - **Embodied Energy** — measured in **MJ/kg** or **GJ/tonne**.
> - **Carbon Footprint (CFP)** — measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent (kgCO₂e)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of R6 — Exhaustive Operational Breakdown

### 🔹 Pillar 1: **REFUSE** (Smartest Strategy)
- **Action:** Decline use of non-essential, harmful, or unsustainable resources.
- **Engineering Application:** Banning single-use plastics, avoiding toxic materials (Pb, Hg, Cd), eliminating over-specification.
- **Why it matters:** Prevents waste *before* it exists — highest leverage point.
- **KTU Buzzword:** "**Refuse is the most preferred waste prevention strategy in the EU Waste Framework Directive (2008/98/EC)**."

### 🔹 Pillar 2: **RETHINK** (Strategic Redesign)
- **Action:** Redesign products, processes, and business models for sustainability.
- **Engineering Application:** Eco-design, Design for Disassembly (DfD), Modular product architecture, Product-as-a-Service (PaaS).
- **Why it matters:** Changes the *system*, not just the artifact.

### 🔹 Pillar 3: **REDUCE** (Resource Efficiency)
- **Action:** Lower the quantity of materials & energy per unit output.
- **Engineering Application:** Lean manufacturing, yield improvement, lightweighting, near-net-shape forming.
- **Formula anchor:** See Section 2.2 below.

### 🔹 Pillar 4: **REUSE** (Direct Reapplication)
- **Action:** Use a product/component again **without major modification** for its original or similar function.
- **Engineering Application:** Refurbished machinery, remanufactured components, reusable containers (e.g., Coca-Cola glass bottles — average **30 reuse cycles**).

### 🔹 Pillar 5: **REPAIR** (Function Restoration)
- **Action:** Restore a damaged product to working condition by replacing only faulty parts.
- **Engineering Application:** Maintenance, overhaul (O/H), spare-part industry, right-to-repair legislation.

### 🔹 Pillar 6: **RECYCLE** (Material Recovery)
- **Action:** Convert waste into new raw materials, with possible **downcycling** (lower quality) or **upcycling** (higher quality).
- **Engineering Application:** Scrap metal remelting, plastic pelletization, e-waste precious-metal recovery.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Metrics for KTU Numerical Questions**

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbol | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recycling Rate** $\quad RR = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total\,input}} \times 100$ | $RR$ | **%** | Primary sustainability KPI |
| 2 | **Material Circularity Indicator** $\quad MCI = 1 - LFI \times F(X)$ | $MCI$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Ellen MacArthur Foundation metric |
| 3 | **Linear Flow Index** $\quad LFI = \dfrac{V+W}{2M + \dfrac{W_{R}}{2} - W_{C}}$ | $LFI$ | dimensionless | Quantifies linearity of flow |
| 4 | **Resource Efficiency** $\quad RE = \dfrac{V_{output}}{M_{input}}$ | $RE$ | ₹/kg or \$/kg | Output value per material input |
| 5 | **Landfill Diversion Rate** $\quad LDR = \dfrac{W_{diverted}}{W_{generated}} \times 100$ | $LDR$ | **%** | Waste management performance |
| 6 | **Virgin Material Savings** $\quad VMS = M_{recycled} \times f_{savings}$ | $VMS$ | **kg** or **tonnes** | Tracks dematerialization |
| 7 | **Energy Recovery Ratio** $\quad ERR = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | $ERR$ | **%** | Energy-from-waste plants |
| 8 | **Carbon Footprint Avoided** $\quad \Delta CFP = M \times EF_{virgin} - M \times EF_{recycled}$ | $\Delta CFP$ | **kgCO₂e** | Climate impact quantification |

Where:
- $M_{recycled}$ = mass recycled, $M_{total\,input}$ = total material input
- $V$ = virgin material input, $W$ = waste output, $M$ = total material flow
- $W_{R}$ = recycled waste, $W_{C}$ = waste collected for recycling
- $f_{savings}$ = fraction of material replaced by recycled content
- $EF_{virgin}$, $EF_{recycled}$ = emission factors (kgCO₂e/kg)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of R3/R6 in Engineering

| Industry Sector | R3/R6 Application | Engineering Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | ELV Directive — 95% reuse/recovery by mass | End-of-Life Vehicle compliance |
| **Electronics** | WEEE Directive, e-waste take-back | Urban mining of rare earths |
| **Steel** | Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) using 100% scrap | 75% energy saving vs. BF-BOF |
| **Aluminium** | Closed-loop can-to-can recycling | 95% energy saving vs. primary Al |
| **Textile** | Reverse logistics for PET bottles → polyester fibre | Circular fashion supply chain |
| **Cement** | Co-processing of RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel) | Alternative fuel in kilns |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> For KTU numerical problems, remember that **recycling aluminium saves up to 95% of energy**, **recycling steel saves ~75%**, and **recycling paper saves ~60%** — these are **memorizable benchmark values** frequently tested.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Material Circularity Indicator (MCI) Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A manufacturing unit consumes **$2000$ kg** of steel in a year. Of this, **$1200$ kg** is virgin material and **$800$ kg** is recycled scrap. Total waste generated is **$300$ kg**, of which **$200$ kg** is recycled internally and **$100$ kg** is sent to landfill. Compute the **Recycling Rate**, **Linear Flow Index**, and **Material Circularity Indicator**.

---

### ✅ Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters

$$V = 1200\ \text{kg} \quad (\text{Virgin material})$$
$$M_{recycled} = 800\ \text{kg} \quad (\text{Recycled input})$$
$$M_{total} = V + M_{recycled} = 1200 + 800 = 2000\ \text{kg}$$
$$W = 300\ \text{kg} \quad (\text{Total waste output})$$
$$W_R = 200\ \text{kg} \quad (\text{Waste recycled})$$
$$W_C = 200\ \text{kg} \quad (\text{Waste collected for recycling})$$

---

### ✅ Step 2: Calculate the Recycling Rate (RR)

$$\begin{aligned}
RR &= \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total\,input}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{800}{2000} \times 100 \\
&= 40\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Only 40% of the total material input comes from recycled sources. The system is **predominantly linear**.

---

### ✅ Step 3: Calculate the Linear Flow Index (LFI)

$$\begin{aligned}
LFI &= \dfrac{V + W}{\,2M + \dfrac{W_R}{2} - W_C\,} \\
&= \dfrac{1200 + 300}{\,2(2000) + \dfrac{200}{2} - 200\,} \\
&= \dfrac{1500}{4000 + 100 - 200} \\
&= \dfrac{1500}{3900} \\
&\approx 0.3846
\end{aligned}$$

---

### ✅ Step 4: Calculate the Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)

> **Note:** The MCI also includes a utility factor $F(X)$ that depends on the **proportion of recycled vs. virgin input** and **lifetime utilization**. For this problem, assume $F(X) = 0.5$ (a common textbook assumption for moderate recycling).

$$\begin{aligned}
MCI &= 1 - LFI \times F(X) \\
&= 1 - 0.3846 \times 0.5 \\
&= 1 - 0.1923 \\
&\approx 0.8077
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** $MCI \approx 0.81$ indicates a **fairly circular** system (close to 1 = highly circular; close to 0 = fully linear).

---

### ✅ Step 5: Comparative Analytical Matrix — R3 vs. R6

| Evaluation Criterion | **R3 Cycle** | **R6 Cycle** | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic Level** | Tactical (mid-stream) | Strategic (upstream + downstream) | **R6 wins** |
| **Number of Actions** | 3 | 6 | **R6 more comprehensive** |
| **Ease of Implementation** | Easy — fits existing processes | Difficult — requires redesign | **R3 wins on simplicity** |
| **Cost of Adoption** | Low–Medium | High (initial redesign) | **R3 wins** |
| **Environmental Impact** | Moderate | High (prevention-focused) | **R6 wins** |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High | **Both important** |
| **Alignment with UN SDG 12** | Partial | Full alignment | **R6 wins** |
| **Origin Framework** | Japan (1990s) | EU Waste Hierarchy + Potting et al. (2017) | R6 is more recent |

---

### ✅ Step 6: Waste Hierarchy Comparison with R9 Concept (Additional Analytical Depth)

> [!NOTE]
> **The R9 Hierarchy** (Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Repurpose, Recycle) is the most comprehensive circular framework. KTU expects students to know that **R3 ⊂ R6 ⊂ R9**, with each level adding strategic depth.

$$\text{R3 (3 actions)} \subset \text{R6 (6 actions)} \subset \text{R9 (9 actions)}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The R6 Cycle as a Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REFUSE - Decline unsustainable resources] --> B[RETHINK - Redesign systems and products]
    B --> C[REDUCE - Minimize material and energy use]
    C --> D[REUSE - Use again without modification]
    D --> E[REPAIR - Restore function via part replacement]
    E --> F[RECYCLE - Reprocess into new raw materials]
    F --> G[New Product Manufacturing]
    G --> A
    H[Virgin Resource Extraction] -.Linear Economy.-> G
    F -.Closed Loop.-> C
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of R3 vs R6 Implementation in a Manufacturing Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R3_Block["R3 CYCLE - OPERATIONAL TACTICAL LAYER"]
        R3A["Reduce Stage\nLean Manufacturing\nMaterial Optimization"]
        R3B["Reuse Stage\nRefurbishment Lines\nReconditioning Cells"]
        R3C["Recycle Stage\nScrap Segregation\nRemelting Units"]
        R3A --> R3B --> R3C
    end
    subgraph R6_Block["R6 CYCLE - STRATEGIC SYSTEMIC LAYER"]
        R6A["Refuse Stage\nSupplier Screening\nToxic Material Ban"]
        R6B["Rethink Stage\nEco-Design Studio\nModular Architecture"]
        R6C["Reduce Stage\nLightweighting Lab\nYield Analytics"]
        R6D["Reuse Stage\nAsset Pool Network\nReverse Logistics"]
        R6E["Repair Stage\nMaintenance Workshop\nSpare Parts Hub"]
        R6F["Recycle Stage\nMaterial Recovery Unit\nUrban Mining"]
        R6A --> R6B --> R6C --> R6D --> R6E --> R6F
    end
    R6_Block --> R3_Block
    R3_Block --> Output["Closed Loop Material Flow\nto Manufacturing Cell"]
    Output --> R6_Block
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing the Right R-Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Material or Product Design Decision] --> Q1{Is the material hazardous or unnecessary?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> REFUSE[Apply REFUSE - Eliminate at source]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Can the system be redesigned?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> RETHINK[Apply RETHINK - Eco-Design]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Can consumption volume be cut?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> REDUCE[Apply REDUCE - Lean & Efficient]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Can the product serve the same function again?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> REUSE[Apply REUSE - Direct Reapplication]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Can a part be replaced to restore function?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> REPAIR[Apply REPAIR - Restoration]
    Q5 -- No --> RECYCLE[Apply RECYCLE - Material Recovery]
    REFUSE --> End[Optimal Sustainability Outcome]
    RETHINK --> End
    REDUCE --> End
    REUSE --> End
    REPAIR --> End
    RECYCLE --> End
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Sankey-Style Material Flow (Block Architecture Fallback)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Virgin Resources 1200 kg] --> M[Manufacturing Process]
    B[Recycled Input 800 kg] --> M
    M --> P[Product Output 1700 kg]
    M --> W1[Recyclable Waste 200 kg]
    M --> W2[Landfill Waste 100 kg]
    W1 --> R[Recycling Loop]
    R --> B
    W2 --> LF[Landfill - Linear Loss]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the R6 cycle. List its six components in the correct order of preference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The R6 cycle is an extended waste management hierarchy applied in sustainable manufacturing to minimize resource depletion and environmental impact. It consists of six actions arranged in descending order of priority:

> **(i) Refuse** — Decline unnecessary or harmful resources.
> **(ii) Rethink** — Redesign products and processes for circularity.
> **(iii) Reduce** — Lower material and energy consumption per unit.
> **(iv) Reuse** — Use a product again for the same or similar function.
> **(v) Repair** — Restore functionality by replacing faulty parts.
> **(vi) Recycle** — Reprocess waste into new raw materials.

The order is critical because actions earlier in the hierarchy consume **less energy** and provide **greater environmental benefit**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Reuse and Recycle with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Reuse** | **Recycle** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Using a product/component again in its **original form** | Reprocessing waste into **new raw material** |
| **Energy Input** | Minimal (cleaning only) | High (remelting, reforming) |
| **Quality Retention** | Same as original | Often lower (downcycling) |
| **Industrial Example** | Refilled glass Coca-Cola bottles (~30 cycles) | PET bottle → polyester fibre |

---

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert ⚠️**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Writing **R3 and R6 in the wrong order** (R6 must start with Refuse, not Reduce).
> - Confusing **Reuse with Repair** — reuse needs no modification; repair involves part replacement.
> - Forgetting **units and numerical substitutions** in MCI problems.
> - Failing to cite **one industrial example** when explaining each R — examiners award 1 mark per example.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)**

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the R3 cycle in detail. How does it support the principles of sustainable manufacturing? Provide one industrial example for each R.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The R3 cycle — comprising **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle** — is the foundational waste hierarchy applied in sustainable manufacturing. It aims to minimize the environmental burden by extracting maximum value from resources before they become waste.

**1. REDUCE (2 marks):**
Reduce is the **first and most preferred** strategy. It focuses on cutting down the quantity of raw materials, energy, and waste generated at the source.
- **Methods:** Lean manufacturing, yield improvement, lightweighting (e.g., aluminum over steel in vehicles), near-net-shape forming.
- **Industrial Example:** Toyota Production System reduces inventory waste through Just-In-Time manufacturing.

**2. REUSE (2 marks):**
Reuse involves using a product or component again **without major alteration** for the same or a similar function.
- **Methods:** Refurbishment lines, returnable containers, second-hand equipment resale.
- **Industrial Example:** Glass bottles in the beverage industry are reused **30+ times** before recycling, saving raw material and energy.

**3. RECYCLE (2 marks):**
Recycling is the **last** in the R3 hierarchy. Waste materials are reprocessed into new raw materials, which may be of **equal, lower (downcycling), or higher (upcycling)** quality.
- **Methods:** Scrap remelting (steel EAF route), plastic pelletization, e-waste precious-metal recovery.
- **Industrial Example:** Recycling aluminum saves up to **95% of energy** compared to producing virgin aluminum from bauxite.

---

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the R6 cycle. How is it an improvement over R3? Give two differences.

**Model Answer:**

**Flowchart Representation (3 marks):**
The R6 cycle follows the sequence: Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Recycle. *(Examiner expects a labelled flowchart here; the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 may be reproduced as a hand-drawn equivalent.)*

**Explanation of each R (2 marks):**
- **Refuse:** Eliminate hazardous or unnecessary inputs (e.g., banning lead in solder — RoHS Directive).
- **Rethink:** Redesign for circularity (e.g., Fairphone modular smartphone design).
- **Reduce:** Less material per product (e.g., concentrated detergents in smaller packs).
- **Reuse:** Same product again (e.g., refillable ink cartridges).
- **Repair:** Restore function (e.g., Bosch power-tool repair service).
- **Recycle:** Material recovery (e.g., e-waste urban mining).

**Two Key Differences from R3 (2 marks):**

| # | **R3** | **R6** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tactical — deals with material already in the system | Strategic — prevents waste at design stage |
| 2 | 3 actions: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle | 6 actions: Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle |

**Conclusion:** R6 is a more holistic extension of R3, embedding prevention at the design stage itself.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)**

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing plant uses **$5000$ kg** of copper annually. **$3500$ kg** is virgin and **$1500$ kg** is recycled scrap. Waste generated is **$600$ kg**, of which **$400$ kg** is sent for recycling and **$200$ kg** to landfill. Compute the **Recycling Rate**, **Linear Flow Index (LFI)**, and **Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)**, assuming utility factor $F(X) = 0.6$. Interpret the results.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$V = 3500\ \text{kg},\quad M_{recycled} = 1500\ \text{kg},\quad M = 5000\ \text{kg}$$
$$W = 600\ \text{kg},\quad W_R = 400\ \text{kg},\quad W_C = 400\ \text{kg},\quad F(X) = 0.6$$

**Step 1: Recycling Rate (RR) (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
RR &= \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1500}{5000} \times 100 \\
&= 30\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> Only 30% of input is recycled — system is **predominantly linear**.

**Step 2: Linear Flow Index (LFI) (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
LFI &= \dfrac{V + W}{\,2M + \dfrac{W_R}{2} - W_C\,} \\
&= \dfrac{3500 + 600}{\,10000 + \dfrac{400}{2} - 400\,} \\
&= \dfrac{4100}{10000 + 200 - 400} \\
&= \dfrac{4100}{9800} \\
&\approx 0.4184
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Material Circularity Indicator (MCI) (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
MCI &= 1 - LFI \times F(X) \\
&= 1 - 0.4184 \times 0.6 \\
&= 1 - 0.2510 \\
&\approx 0.7490
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- $RR = 30\%$ → only **moderate** recycling adoption.
- $MCI \approx 0.75$ → indicates a **fairly circular** system. Higher MCI closer to 1 would reflect a near-closed-loop circular economy.

---

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Refuse** and **Rethink** as the most strategic R-actions in sustainable manufacturing. Support your answer with two real-world case studies.

**Model Answer:**

**1. REFUSE — Strategic Importance (2.5 marks):**
Refuse is the **highest-priority** action in the R6 hierarchy because it eliminates waste **before** it is generated. By refusing hazardous, toxic, or unnecessary materials at the procurement stage, manufacturers avoid the cost and complexity of downstream waste handling.
- **Energy Saved:** Zero processing energy for refused material.
- **Cost Saved:** Procurement, storage, and disposal costs eliminated.
- **Case Study 1:** The **RoHS Directive (EU, 2003)** restricted the use of lead, mercury, cadmium, and certain flame retardants in electronics. Refusing these substances at the design stage eliminated complex end-of-pipe treatment.

**2. RETHINK — Strategic Importance (2.5 marks):**
Rethink promotes **Design for Environment (DfE)** principles, embedding circularity into the product from inception. It involves modular design, design for disassembly, and Product-as-a-Service business models.
- **Case Study 2:** **Fairphone (Netherlands)** redesigned smartphones with **modular components** that can be easily replaced, repaired, or upgraded — extending product life from 2 years to over 5 years.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | **Refuse** | **Rethink** |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Procurement / Sourcing | Design / Conception |
| Focus | What NOT to use | How to design better |
| Sustainability Impact | Eliminates hazardous input | Extends product lifespan |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Refuse and Rethink are upstream interventions that **prevent waste at the root**, making them far more effective than downstream actions like Recycle, which merely manage waste after creation.

---

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ **R3 = Reduce, Reuse, Recycle** (Tactical / Operational layer).
- ✅ **R6 = Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle** (Strategic / Systemic layer).
- ✅ **Order of priority matters** — earlier R's have higher sustainability leverage and lower energy demand.
- ✅ **Recycle is ALWAYS the last resort** in both R3 and R6 — examiners test this!
- ✅ **MCI** is bounded between **0 (fully linear)** and **1 (fully circular)**.
- ✅ **Energy benchmarks:** Aluminium recycling saves **~95%**, Steel **~75%**, Paper **~60%** energy.
- ✅ **Formula sheet (MCI, LFI, RR)** must be memorized — frequently asked in KTU Part B.
- ✅ **Industrial examples carry 1 mark each** — always cite (Toyota, Fairphone, Coca-Cola, RoHS, etc.).
- ✅ **R6 ⊃ R3** — all R3 actions are present in R6 plus three additional strategic ones.
- ✅ The **EU Waste Framework Directive (2008/98/EC)** legally establishes this hierarchy.
- ✅ **UN SDG 12** (Responsible Consumption and Production) is the global policy anchor for R3/R6.
- ✅ **Refuse > Rethink > Reduce > Reuse > Repair > Recycle** — this sequence is **non-negotiable** in answers.
- ✅ Distinguish clearly: **Reuse (no modification) ≠ Repair (part replacement) ≠ Recycle (material destruction & reconstitution).**

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Impact Assessment Methods - CML, EI 95 and 99
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT631 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Environmental Impact Assessment Methods - CML, EI 95 and 99 ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Methods** in the context of **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** refer to the **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** phase as defined in **ISO 14040** and **ISO 14044**. Specifically, the methods **CML**, **Eco-indicator 95 (EI 95)**, and **Eco-indicator 99 (EI 99)** are standardized, scientifically-validated methodologies used to translate the **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** emissions and resource extractions into quantifiable environmental impact scores. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > LCIA methods are mandatory elements for evaluating the **environmental performance** of products, processes, and manufacturing systems under the **ISO 14040/14044** framework. #### Formal Definitions: | Acronym | Full Form | Origin | Type | |---------|-----------|--------|------| | **CML** | **C**enter of **M**ilieu (Environmental) Studies, **L**eiden University | Leiden University, Netherlands (1992, updated 2001) | Problem-oriented, **midpoint & endpoint** | | **EI 95** | **E**co-**I**ndicator **95** | PRé Consultants, Netherlands (1995) | Damage-oriented, **endpoint** | | **EI 99** | **E**co-**I**ndicator **99** | PRé Consultants, RIVM, TNO (1999) | Damage-oriented, **endpoint** with cultural perspectives | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Translator" Analogy:** > Imagine the Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) is a **shopping list of pollutants**: 5 kg of CO₂, 0.2 kg of SO₂, 1.5 kg of NOₓ, 200 MJ of fossil fuel, etc. Each item is measured in a different "currency" (kilograms, megajoules, cubic meters). > > **Problem:** How do you know whether 5 kg of CO₂ is "worse" or "better" than 0.2 kg of SO₂? > > **Solution:** LCIA methods act like a **currency exchange booth** at an airport. > - **CML (Midpoint approach)** is like converting all currencies to a common intermediate unit (e.g., kg CO₂-equivalent) — a "translation hub" that groups related emissions (e.g., all greenhouse gases become GWP100). > - **EI 95 / EI 99 (Endpoint approach)** is like adding up the **final damage** in your wallet — collapsing all impacts into a single "Eco-score" representing actual harm to Human Health, Ecosystem Quality, and Resources. ### 1.3 Why Three Methods? The reason three distinct methods exist stems from a fundamental design trade-off: - **Midpoint (CML-midpoint):** Higher scientific certainty, less subjective, but harder to interpret. "We emitted 20 kg CO₂-eq of global warming potential." - **Endpoint (CML-endpoint / EI 95 / EI 99):** Higher interpretability (you can tell the user "this causes 0.0001 DALY of human health damage"), but requires additional modeling assumptions and value choices. - **Damage Categories:** EI 99 explicitly incorporates **cultural/political perspectives** (Egalitarian, Hierarchist, Individualist), making it policy-sensitive. ### 1.4 Key Terminology > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Glossary (KTU Board Examiner expects exact terms):** > - **Characterization Factor (CF):** A scientific coefficient that converts LCI data to a common impact unit. > - **Midpoint (Problem-oriented):** An impact category located along the cause-effect chain (e.g., climate change, acidification). > - **Endpoint (Damage-oriented):** The final consequence on Areas of Protection: Human Health, Ecosystem Quality, Resources. > - **Areas of Protection (AoP):** Three ultimate concerns of environmental policy: human welfare, natural environment, and natural resources. > - **Eco-indicator Score:** A single numerical value (typically in **Points/mPt**) representing aggregated environmental damage. > - **Damage Function:** A model linking emissions → midpoint → endpoint damage. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Mandatory LCIA Steps (Per ISO 14042 / 14044) All three methods — CML, EI 95, and EI 99 — follow the **ISO 14042/14044** framework with the following mandatory and optional elements: 1. **Classification** — Assigning LCI flows to relevant impact categories. 2. **Characterization** — Multiplying LCI data by Characterization Factors. 3. **Normalization** *(optional)* — Dividing impact by a reference value (e.g., total annual emissions of a country). 4. **Weighting** *(optional)* — Aggregating across impact categories using value-based weights. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** CML is officially a **midpoint problem-oriented** method. EI 95 and EI 99 are **endpoint damage-oriented** methods. This is the **#1 most-tested KTU question** in this module. ### 2.2 The CML Method (Leiden, 2001) The **CML 2001** method is a problem-oriented (midpoint) approach developed by the **Institute of Environmental Sciences (CML), Leiden University**. It is the most widely used LCIA method globally and forms the basis of many commercial LCA software tools (SimaPro, GaBi, openLCA). #### 2.2.1 Impact Categories in CML | Impact Category | Abbreviation | Unit (Characterization Reference) | |-----------------|--------------|-----------------------------------| | Abiotic Resource Depletion | **ADP** | kg Sb-eq (antimony equivalent) | | Acidification Potential | **AP** | kg SO₂-eq | | Eutrophication Potential | **EP** | kg PO₄³⁻-eq | | Freshwater Aquatic Ecotoxicity | **FAETP** | kg 1,4-DCB-eq | | Global Warming Potential (GWP 100) | **GWP** | kg CO₂-eq | | Human Toxicity Potential | **HTP** | kg 1,4-DCB-eq | | Marine Aquatic Ecotoxicity | **MAETP** | kg 1,4-DCB-eq | | Ozone Layer Depletion | **ODP** | kg CFC-11-eq | | Photochemical Ozone Creation | **POCP** | kg C₂H₄-eq (ethylene) | | Terrestrial Ecotoxicity | **TETP** | kg 1,4-DCB-eq | #### 2.2.2 CML Characterization Formula (Midpoint) The fundamental calculation at the midpoint level is: $$ \text{Impact}_j = \sum_i \text{LCI}_i \times \text{CF}_{i,j} $$ Where: - $\text{Impact}_j$ = Magnitude of impact category $j$ (e.g., kg CO₂-eq for GWP). - $\text{LCI}_i$ = Mass (kg) of life cycle inventory flow $i$. - $\text{CF}_{i,j}$ = Characterization factor of flow $i$ with respect to impact category $j$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Application:** The CML GWP100 characterization factor for **methane (CH₄)** is **CF = 25 kg CO₂-eq/kg CH₄**. This is the official **IPCC AR5 (100-year)** value used in carbon footprinting of manufacturing emissions. ### 2.3 The Eco-indicator 95 Method Developed in **1995** by **PRé Consultants** (with Mark Goedkoop as lead author), **Eco-indicator 95** was the first widely-adopted damage-oriented method in Europe. It was created specifically for **designer-friendly** product comparison (e.g., the Philips Product Ecology Card). #### 2.3.1 The Three Damage Categories (Areas of Protection) EI 95 explicitly uses three Areas of Protection (AoP): 1. **Human Health** — measured in **DALY** (*Disability-Adjusted Life Years*), which is a standardized WHO metric combining years of life lost and years lived with disability. 2. **Ecosystem Quality** — measured in **PDF·m²·yr** (*Potentially Disappeared Fraction of plant species × m² × year*). 3. **Resources** — measured in **MJ surplus** (extra energy needed in the future to extract increasingly scarce minerals and fossil fuels). > [!NOTE] > **Why these three metrics?** They represent the three fundamental things humans care about: their own health, the natural environment, and access to natural resources. #### 2.3.2 The Eco-indicator 95 Workflow 1. LCI emissions → mapped to **100 impact categories** (substance-to-midpoint). 2. Midpoint → translated to **damage categories** using damage functions. 3. Each damage category result is multiplied by a **weighting factor** to yield a single **Eco-indicator point (Pt)**. 4. The final score is expressed in **millipoints (mPt)** so a typical product scores 10–1000 mPt. #### 2.3.3 The Weighting Methodology (EI 95) The weights in EI 95 were derived from a **panel of 30 experts** using a **distance-to-target** method, i.e., how far the current environmental load is from politically-set sustainability targets. $$ \text{Weight}_j = \frac{1}{\text{Target}_j} $$ Where $\text{Target}_j$ is the policy-set sustainability target for impact category $j$. ### 2.4 The Eco-indicator 99 Method The **EI 99** was developed in **1997–1999** as a major revision by **PRé Consultants**, **RIVM** (Dutch National Institute for Public Health and Environment), **TNO** (Dutch research organization), and the **Design Center**. It introduced three major innovations over EI 95. #### 2.4.1 The Three Cultural Perspectives (Hallmarks of EI 99) EI 99 introduced a **time-horizon** and **cultural perspective** dimension — three archetypes based on the work of cultural theorist **Aaron Wildavsky**: | Perspective | Time Horizon | Risk Attitude | Trust in Technology | Use Case | |-------------|--------------|---------------|---------------------|----------| | **Individualist** (I) | Short (≤ 20 yr) | Risk-tolerant | High | US business-as-usual view | | **Hierarchist** (H) | Medium (50–100 yr) | Balanced (official IPCC policy) | Moderate | Default (most policies) | | **Egalitarian** (E) | Long (≥ 500 yr) | Risk-averse | Low | Precautionary principle | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **Hierarchist (H)** perspective is the **default** in EI 99 and aligns with **IPCC**, **CML**, and most European policy documents. #### 2.4.2 The EI 99 Damage Categories Same as EI 95 (Human Health, Ecosystem Quality, Resources) but with: - **Updated damage models** (more scientific rigor). - **Conjoint analysis** weighting (instead of distance-to-target). - **Sub-categorization** within each damage category. #### 2.4.3 The Conjoint Analysis Weighting (EI 99) > [!NOTE] > **Conjoint Analysis** is a statistical survey technique where respondents (a panel of 365 experts) rank hypothetical environmental scenarios. Trade-offs are used to derive **utility-based weights** rather than distance-to-target. $$ W_j = \frac{\partial U / \partial X_j}{\sum_k \partial U / \partial X_k} $$ Where $U$ is the respondent's utility function and $X_j$ is the damage category score. #### 2.4.4 EI 99 Final Score Formula $$ \text{Eco-indicator 99 Score} = \sum_{j=1}^{3} \left( \text{Damage}_j \times W_j \right) $$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for CML, EI 95, and EI 99. Memorize the **units** in particular — they are test-favorites. | Method | Level | Core Formula | Output Unit | |--------|-------|--------------|-------------| | CML (Midpoint) | Midpoint | $\text{Impact}_j = \sum_i \text{LCI}_i \cdot \text{CF}_{i,j}$ | kg reference-eq (e.g., kg CO₂-eq) | | CML (Endpoint) | Endpoint | $\text{Damage}_A = \sum_j \text{Impact}_j \cdot \text{CF}_{j \to A}$ | DALY, PDF·m²·yr, MJ | | EI 95 | Endpoint | $\text{Score} = \sum_A \text{Damage}_A \cdot W_A$ | Points (Pt) or millipoints (mPt) | | EI 99 | Endpoint | $\text{Score} = \sum_A \sum_p \text{Damage}_{A,p} \cdot W_{A,p}$ | Points (Pt) or millipoints (mPt) | | Normalization | Optional | $N_j = \text{Impact}_j / \text{Ref}_j$ | Dimensionless | | Weighting | Optional | $S = \sum_j N_j \cdot W_j$ | Composite score | ### 2.6 Comparative Engineering Utility | Method | Best Used For | Avoid When | |--------|---------------|------------| | **CML Midpoint** | Carbon footprinting, EPDs (Environmental Product Declarations), scientific reports | You need a single number for management decisions | | **CML Endpoint** | Full LCA studies with policy implications | Quick screening | | **EI 95** | Legacy product comparison studies, early-stage eco-design | Modern studies (superseded by EI 99) | | **EI 99** | Designer-friendly communication, eco-design, DfE (Design for Environment) | Strictly scientific/policy reports requiring IPCC-aligned values | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Midpoint → Endpoint Translation (CML-style) The translation from midpoint impact category $j$ to endpoint damage category $A$ involves a **damage function** $f_{j \to A}$ and a **damage characterization factor** $D_{j \to A}$. **Step 1:** Compute the midpoint impact. $$ \text{Impact}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \text{CF}_{i,j} $$ Where: - $m_i$ = mass of substance $i$ released (kg). - $\text{CF}_{i,j}$ = characterization factor of substance $i$ for midpoint impact $j$. **Step 2:** Map the midpoint to the relevant damage category (e.g., GWP → Human Health via climate-change-induced disease and heat stress). $$ \text{Damage}_A = \sum_{j \in A} \text{Impact}_j \cdot D_{j \to A} $$ Where $D_{j \to A}$ is the damage characterization factor linking midpoint $j$ to AoP $A$ (units: DALY per kg reference-eq, or PDF·m²·yr per kg reference-eq). **Step 3:** Apply weighting (EI 95 / EI 99 only). $$ S = \sum_A \text{Damage}_A \cdot W_A $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Steel Production > [!NOTE] > **Scenario (from KTU-style problem sets):** A small manufacturing unit produces **1 tonne (1000 kg) of crude steel** via the **basic oxygen furnace (BOF)** route. The LCI yields the following emissions per tonne of steel: > - CO₂: 1850 kg > - SO₂: 1.4 kg > - NOₓ: 0.9 kg (as NO₂) > - CH₄: 0.05 kg > - Particulates (PM10): 0.6 kg We will compute the **CML midpoint impacts** for the first three categories (GWP, AP, POCP). #### Step 1: List Characterization Factors (CML 2001, 100-year) | Substance | CF for GWP (kg CO₂-eq / kg) | CF for AP (kg SO₂-eq / kg) | CF for POCP (kg C₂H₄-eq / kg) | |-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------| | CO₂ | 1.00 | 0 | 0 | | SO₂ | 0 | 1.00 | 0 | | NO₂ | 0 | 0.70 | 0.028 | | CH₄ | 25.00 | 0 | 0.007 | #### Step 2: Calculate GWP $$ \text{GWP} = (1850)(1.00) + (1.4)(0) + (0.9)(0) + (0.05)(25.00) $$ $$ \text{GWP} = 1850 + 0 + 0 + 1.25 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{GWP} = 1851.25 \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq}} $$ #### Step 3: Calculate AP $$ \text{AP} = (1850)(0) + (1.4)(1.00) + (0.9)(0.70) + (0.05)(0) $$ $$ \text{AP} = 0 + 1.4 + 0.63 + 0 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{AP} = 2.03 \;\text{kg SO}_2\text{-eq}} $$ #### Step 4: Calculate POCP $$ \text{POCP} = (1850)(0) + (1.4)(0) + (0.9)(0.028) + (0.05)(0.007) $$ $$ \text{POCP} = 0 + 0 + 0.0252 + 0.00035 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{POCP} = 0.02555 \;\text{kg C}_2\text{H}_4\text{-eq}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: Eco-indicator 99 Damage Calculation **Continuing the steel example,** suppose we want to compute the **EI 99 single score** for the GWP contribution only. We use the following (illustrative) EI 99 damage characterization factors for the **Hierarchist (H)** perspective: - $D_{\text{GWP} \to \text{Human Health}} = 3.1 \times 10^{-4}$ DALY per kg CO₂-eq - $D_{\text{GWP} \to \text{Ecosystem Quality}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ PDF·m²·yr per kg CO₂-eq - $D_{\text{GWP} \to \text{Resources}}$ (not applicable for GWP; ≈ 0) EI 99 H weights (default from literature): - $W_{\text{Human Health}} = 300$ mPt/DALY - $W_{\text{Ecosystem Quality}} = 400$ mPt/(PDF·m²·yr) - $W_{\text{Resources}} = 200$ mPt/MJ surplus **Step 1:** Compute damage in Human Health (HH). $$ \text{Damage}_{\text{HH}} = 1851.25 \times 3.1 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Damage}_{\text{HH}} = 0.5739 \;\text{DALY}} $$ **Step 2:** Compute damage in Ecosystem Quality (EQ). $$ \text{Damage}_{\text{EQ}} = 1851.25 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Damage}_{\text{EQ}} = 4.628 \;\text{PDF}\cdot m^2 \cdot yr} $$ **Step 3:** Apply weights to convert to millipoints. $$ \text{Score}_{\text{HH}} = 0.5739 \times 300 = 172.17 \;\text{mPt} $$ $$ \text{Score}_{\text{EQ}} = 4.628 \times 400 = 1851.2 \;\text{mPt} $$ **Step 4:** Sum for the GWP-only EI 99 score. $$ \boxed{S_{\text{GWP}} = 172.17 + 1851.2 = 2023.37 \;\text{mPt}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The 1 tonne of steel contributes ~2023 mPt (≈ 2 Pt) of global warming damage alone. A typical smartphone is about 5000–8000 mPt in total — this is why steel is considered high-impact. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: CML vs. EI 95 vs. EI 99 | Feature | CML 2001 (Midpoint) | CML 2001 (Endpoint) | EI 95 | EI 99 | |---------|---------------------|---------------------|-------|-------| | **Year** | 1992 / 2001 | 2001 | 1995 | 1999 | | **Approach** | Midpoint | Endpoint | Endpoint | Endpoint | | **Number of Categories** | ~10 | 3 | 3 | 3 (with 11 sub) | | **Areas of Protection** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Damage Function** | No | Yes (simple) | Yes | Yes (advanced) | | **Weighting Method** | Optional (external) | Optional (external) | Distance-to-target | Conjoint analysis | | **Cultural Perspective** | No | No | No | **Yes (I, H, E)** | | **CF Source** | Scientific consensus | Same | Panel of 30 | RIVM/TNO/Design Center | | **Software Support** | SimaPro, GaBi, openLCA | Same | SimaPro | SimaPro, GaBi | --- ### 3.5 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Method > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Exam Answers (2-mark / 3-mark questions):** > Use this decision logic when explaining which method to choose: > 1. **Goal = Carbon footprint?** → Use CML GWP100 midpoint. > 2. **Goal = Designer-friendly single score?** → Use EI 99. > 3. **Goal = Long-term / precautionary view?** → Use EI 99 Egalitarian. > 4. **Goal = Scientific report (IPCC-aligned)?** → Use CML Hierarchist perspective. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CML LCIA Flow (Problem-Oriented / Midpoint) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[LCI Inventory Data] --> B[Classification] B --> C[Characterization
Multiply by CF] C --> D{Optional Steps} D --> E[Normalization
Divide by Ref Value] E --> F[Weighting
Apply W_j] F --> G[Midpoint Impact Scores
kg CO2-eq, kg SO2-eq, etc.] G --> H[Damage Assessment
Link to Areas of Protection] H --> I[Endpoint Damage Scores
DALY, PDF m2 yr, MJ] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2 style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c ``` ### 4.2 EI 99 Architecture with Cultural Perspectives ```mermaid flowchart TD A[LCI Data] --> B[Classification
100 Substances] B --> C[Characterization

in mPt] style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd style J fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ### 4.3 Cause-Effect Chain (Cause → Midpoint → Endpoint → AoP) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Emission
