topics.title: Lifecycle of Products/Services from Development to End of Life Stages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT631 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lifecycle of Products/Services from Development to End of Life Stages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** — The complete sequence of stages a manufactured product passes through, from the extraction of raw materials, through design, production, distribution, use, and finally to its end-of-life (EOL) treatment. In the context of sustainable manufacturing, the PLC is also called the **Product Value Chain** or **Cradle-to-Grave** continuum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Life Cycle (SLC)** — The structured progression of a service offering through four primary phases: **Concept & Strategy → Service Design & Development → Service Transition/Launch → Service Operation & Continuous Improvement → Retirement**. Unlike a physical product, a service is intangible, co-produced with the customer, and consumed at the point of delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> **End-of-Life (EOL) Stage** — The terminal phase of a product's life cycle in which the manufacturer, user, or third-party recovery network decides among the **5 R-strategies**: *Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, and Remanufacture/Redesign*, as opposed to disposal (landfill/incineration).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **smartphone** you are using right now.

- **Conception / Birth**: Mineral ores are mined in Africa; engineers design the circuit board in California.
- **Growth / Youth**: The phone is assembled in a Foxconn plant, packaged, shipped worldwide, marketed — its "life" accelerates.
- **Maturity / Adulthood**: You use it for 2–3 years. Software updates keep it relevant.
- **Decline / Old Age**: Battery degrades, screen cracks, newer models emerge.
- **Death**: You discard it.

Now the question a **sustainable manufacturer** asks is: *At "Death," can the phone's body, lithium, cobalt, and glass be reborn into a new phone?* If **yes**, then we have moved from a **Cradle-to-Grave** model to a **Cradle-to-Cradle** (C2C) loop — the philosophical heart of Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical / Standard Metrics Used in This Module**
> - **Product Lifetime (T)**: Average operational lifespan of a product, measured in **years**, **kilometres**, or **operating cycles**.
> - **Recycling Rate (η)**: Dimensionless ratio (0–1) or percentage of input material recovered at EOL.
> - **Net Present Value of Lifecycle Cost (LCC)**: Measured in **INR/USD** over the full horizon.
> - **Embodied Energy (EE)**: Measured in **MJ/kg** or **kWh/unit**.

---

## 1.3 The Six Universal Stages of Product/Service Lifecycle

Every manufactured artifact and engineered service traverses the following six gates:

| Stage # | Stage Name | Dominant Activity | Sustainability Lens |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Raw Material Acquisition** | Mining, refining, agriculture | Resource depletion, biodiversity, embodied energy |
| **2** | **Design & Development** | CAD/CAE, prototyping, DfE | Design for Environment, modularity, life extension |
| **3** | **Manufacturing / Production** | Forming, machining, assembly | Energy intensity, emissions, waste |
| **4** | **Distribution & Logistics** | Packaging, transport, warehousing | Carbon footprint of freight, reverse logistics |
| **5** | **Use / Service Delivery** | Operation, maintenance, repair | Energy in-use, durability, upgradeability |
| **6** | **End-of-Life (EOL)** | Reuse, Remanufacture, Recycle, Recover, Dispose | Circularity, hazardous handling, landfill burden |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this Module Matters (KTU 2024 / NEP 2020 Context)**: Modern KTU assessment treats the lifecycle as an *integrated decision-making framework* — not just a post-sale cleanup. Approximately **60–70% of a product's total environmental footprint is locked in at the Design stage**, making lifecycle thinking a **preventive** rather than reactive discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Logic Chain

Sustainable lifecycle thinking is built on five interlocking logic blocks:

- **Logic Block 1 — Linear vs. Cyclic Paradigm**
  - *Why*: Industrial-age manufacturing was linear (Take → Make → Dispose), driving resource depletion.
  - *How*: Lifecycle science reframes every stage as either a **producer of value** *or* a **source of feedback** for the next cycle.

- **Logic Block 2 — The 5 R-Strategies of EOL (Hierarchical)**
  Most preferred ➡ Least preferred from a sustainability standpoint:
  - **R1 – Refuse/Reduce** (least material used in first place)
  - **R2 – Reuse** (entire product re-enters service)
  - **R3 – Repair / Refurbish / Remanufacture** (component-level reincarnation)
  - **R4 – Recycle** (material-level reprocessing)
  - **R5 – Recover** (energy recovery via incineration with energy capture)
  - **Disposal** (landfill — the absolute last resort)

- **Logic Block 3 — Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) vs. Cradle-to-Grave (C2G)**
  - *Cradle-to-Grave*: Raw material ➡ Product ➡ Waste (open loop).
  - *Cradle-to-Cradle*: All outputs become *biological nutrients* or *technical nutrients* that loop back (closed loop).

- **Logic Block 4 — Service vs. Product Differentiation**
  Services have **intangibility**, **inseparability** (produced and consumed simultaneously), **variability**, and **perishability**. Their lifecycle emphasizes **process quality** rather than material flow.

- **Logic Block 5 — Reverse Logistics**
  - *Why*: Without it, EOL recovery is impossible at scale.
  - *How*: A structured flow of products *backward* through the supply chain — from customer back to manufacturer or third-party recovery hubs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Model | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $T_{LC} = T_{design} + T_{mfg} + T_{dist} + T_{use} + T_{EOL}$ | Total product life time | **years** |
| **F2** | $LCC = C_{acq} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_{op,i}}{(1+r)^{i}} + \frac{C_{EOL}}{(1+r)^{n}} - \frac{R_{res}}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | Lifecycle Cost (discounted) | **INR/USD** |
| **F3** | $\eta_{recycle} = \frac{M_{recycled}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Material recycling efficiency | **%**, dimensionless |
| **F4** | $\eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{total\,at\,EOL}} \times 100\%$ | Aggregate EOL recovery rate | **%**, dimensionless |
| **F5** | $N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p}\cdot e^{-(p+q)t}}$ | Bass Diffusion — cumulative adopters | persons/units; **p** = innovation coeff., **q** = imitation coeff., **m** = market potential |
| **F6** | $MCI = 1 - F_{WM} \cdot \frac{L_{V}}{2L_{avg}} + \frac{F_{R}}{2}$ | Material Circularity Indicator (Ellen MacArthur) | $0 \le MCI \le 1$ |
| **F7** | $EE_{total} = \sum_{j} M_j \cdot EE_j$ | Total Embodied Energy of product | **MJ** or **kWh** |
| **F8** | $CF_{logistics} = \sum_{k} m_k \cdot d_k \cdot EF_k$ | Logistics carbon footprint | **kg CO₂e**; m = mass, d = distance, EF = emission factor |
| **F9** | $R_{score} = \frac{R_{reuse} + R_{repair} + R_{reman} + R_{recycle} + R_{recover}}{5}$ | Composite R-hierarchy score | normalized **0–1** |
| **F10** | $\tau_{service} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{fail}}$ | Mean Time To Service Failure (MTTF) | **hours** or **cycles** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Valuation Note**: In F2 (Life Cycle Cost), the discount rate **r** must always be expressed as a **decimal (e.g., 0.08 for 8 %)**, NOT as a percentage. A frequent error in KTU scripts is mixing units of r across sub-parts of a problem.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

Lifecycle thinking is operationalised across industries:

- **Automotive**: Volkswagen's "End-of-Life Vehicles" directive (EU ELV) mandates 95 % recovery rate.
- **Electronics**: Apple's **Daisy** disassembly robot reclaims 14 materials from iPhones.
- **Services**: Hospitals use **service blueprints** to map patient journeys from admission to discharge.
- **Energy**: Solar PV panels require lifecycle assessment because manufacturing is energy-intensive even though use-phase is clean.
- **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods)**: Unilever's "Sustainable Living Plan" applies lifecycle thinking to detergent powders.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Bass Diffusion Model (Product Adoption Lifecycle)

> [!NOTE]
> The Bass Diffusion Model is the **canonical** mathematical representation of how a new product spreads through a population — i.e., it models the **Sales Curve** of a product's life cycle. KTU frequently tests this because it links lifecycle *theory* to *quantitative* analysis.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:

- $N(t)$ = cumulative number of adopters by time $t$
- $m$ = total market potential (maximum possible adopters)
- $p$ = coefficient of *innovation* (external influence, e.g., advertising)
- $q$ = coefficient of *imitation* (internal influence, e.g., word of mouth)
- $f(t)$ = fraction of market adopted at time $t$ given not-yet-adopted

### Step 2 — Set Up the Adoption Probability

The probability that an individual who has *not* yet adopted the product will do so at time $t$ is given by Bass's hypothesis:

$$
f(t) \;=\; p \;+\; q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first term ($p$) captures spontaneous innovation-driven adoption (constant external pressure). The second term ($q \cdot N(t)/m$) captures imitation, which scales with how many people have *already* adopted — the "bandwagon" effect.

### Step 3 — Convert to a Differential Equation

The instantaneous rate of new adoptions equals the rate at which the remaining non-adopters convert. Mathematically:

$$
\frac{dN(t)}{dt} \;=\; \left[\, p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m} \,\right] \cdot \bigl[\, m - N(t) \,\bigr]
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $(m - N(t))$ is the *remaining* untapped market, and $[p + q \cdot N(t)/m]$ is the probability each remaining individual adopts in the next instant.

### Step 4 — Substitute $f(t) = N(t)/m$

Define $f(t) = N(t)/m$, so $N(t) = m \cdot f(t)$ and $dN/dt = m \cdot df/dt$.

Substituting into Step 3:

$$
m \cdot \frac{df}{dt} \;=\; \bigl[\, p + q \cdot f \,\bigr] \cdot m \cdot (1 - f)
$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$
\frac{df}{dt} \;=\; \bigl[\, p + q \cdot f \,\bigr] \cdot (1 - f)
$$

### Step 5 — Separate Variables

$$
\frac{df}{(p + q f)(1 - f)} \;=\; dt
$$

### Step 6 — Partial-Fraction Decomposition

We seek constants $A$ and $B$ such that:

$$
\frac{1}{(p + q f)(1 - f)} \;=\; \frac{A}{p + q f} \;+\; \frac{B}{1 - f}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $(p + q f)(1 - f)$:

$$
1 \;=\; A (1 - f) \;+\; B (p + q f)
$$

**Sub-condition (i)** — set $f = 1$:

$$
1 \;=\; B \cdot p \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; B \;=\; \frac{1}{p}
$$

**Sub-condition (ii)** — set $f = -p/q$:

$$
1 \;=\; A \left(1 + \frac{p}{q}\right) \;=\; A \cdot \frac{p+q}{q} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A \;=\; \frac{q}{p+q}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{(p + q f)(1 - f)} \;=\; \frac{q/(p+q)}{p + q f} \;+\; \frac{1/p}{1 - f}
$$

### Step 7 — Integrate Both Sides

$$
\int \frac{q/(p+q)}{p + q f}\, df \;+\; \int \frac{1/p}{1 - f}\, df \;=\; \int dt
$$

The left side integrates as follows:

- First integral: $\displaystyle \int \frac{df}{p + q f} = \frac{1}{q} \ln(p + q f)$
- Second integral: $\displaystyle \int \frac{df}{1 - f} = -\ln(1 - f)$

Combining the constants:

$$
\frac{1}{p+q} \ln(p + q f) \;-\; \frac{1}{p} \ln(1 - f) \;=\; t \;+\; C
$$

### Step 8 — Apply Initial Condition

At $t = 0$, no one has adopted yet, so $f(0) = 0$:

$$
\frac{1}{p+q} \ln(p) \;-\; \frac{1}{p} \ln(1) \;=\; 0 + C
$$

Since $\ln(1) = 0$:

$$
C \;=\; \frac{\ln p}{p+q}
$$

### Step 9 — Solve for $f(t)$ Explicitly

$$
\frac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left(\frac{p + q f}{p}\right) \;=\; t \;+\; \frac{1}{p} \ln(1 - f)
$$

Multiply both sides by $p(p+q)$:

$$
p \,\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{q f}{p}\right) \;-\; (p+q) \,\ln(1 - f) \;=\; p(p+q)\, t
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
\frac{\bigl(1 + \tfrac{q f}{p}\bigr)^{p}}{(1 - f)^{p+q}} \;=\; e^{p(p+q)t}
$$

Rearranging and solving for $f(t)$:

$$
f(t) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \tfrac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}
$$

### Step 10 — Re-introduce $N(t)$

$$
N(t) \;=\; m \cdot f(t) \;=\; m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \tfrac{q}{p}\, e^{-(p+q)t}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boxed Final Result (Bass Diffusion — Cumulative Adopters):**
>
> $$\boxed{\; N(t) \;=\; m \cdot \dfrac{\,1 - e^{-(p+q)t}\,}{\,1 + \dfrac{q}{p}\, e^{-(p+q)t}\,} \;}$$
>
> This is the **S-curve** of cumulative adoption. Differentiating it gives the **bell-shaped sales curve** of the classic Product Life Cycle (Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline).

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Net Present Value of Lifecycle Cost

The Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of a product owned over $n$ years, with annual operating cost $C_{op,i}$ in year $i$ and a one-time EOL cost/revenue $C_{EOL}$ and salvage $R_{res}$ at year $n$, with discount rate $r$, is derived as:

$$
LCC \;=\; C_{acq} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_{op,i}}{(1+r)^{i}} \;+\; \frac{C_{EOL}}{(1+r)^{n}} \;-\; \frac{R_{res}}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each future cash flow is divided by $(1+r)^i$ to bring it to *present value*. Acquisition is already at present ($t=0$). EOL and residual value both occur at $t=n$, so they share the discount factor $(1+r)^n$.

**Numerical Example**: A machine costs **₹5,00,000** to acquire. Annual operating cost is **₹80,000** for **6 years**. Discount rate is **8 %**. EOL dismantling cost is **₹30,000** and salvage value is **₹50,000**. Find LCC.

$$
LCC = 500000 + 80000 \cdot \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{6}\frac{1}{(1.08)^i}}_{A} + \frac{30000}{(1.08)^6} - \frac{50000}{(1.08)^6}
$$

First, evaluate the geometric series $A$:

$$
A = \frac{1}{0.08}\left[1 - \frac{1}{(1.08)^6}\right] = 12.5 \cdot [1 - 0.6302] = 12.5 \cdot 0.3698 = 4.6225
$$

So:

$$
LCC = 500000 + 80000(4.6225) + \frac{30000 - 50000}{1.5869}
$$

$$
LCC = 500000 + 369800 + \frac{-20000}{1.5869}
$$

$$
LCC = 500000 + 369800 - 12603.6 \;=\; \mathbf{₹\,8,57,196.4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip**: Always state *units* of the answer and check that $R_{res} > C_{EOL}$ does not accidentally make LCC smaller than acquisition cost — flag such contradictions in your model solution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)

The Ellen MacArthur Foundation's MCI quantifies how *circular* a product's material flow is.

$$
MCI \;=\; 1 \;-\; F_{WM} \cdot \frac{L_{V}}{2\,L_{avg}} \;+\; \frac{F_{R}}{2}
$$

Where:

- $F_{WM}$ = fraction of input feedstock from *virgin* (primary) raw material
- $L_{V}$ = lifespan of the virgin material (proxy = product life)
- $L_{avg}$ = industry-average product lifespan
- $F_{R}$ = fraction of feedstock from *recycled* sources

**Interpretation**:
- $MCI = 0$ → Fully linear (no circularity)
- $MCI = 1$ → Fully circular (only recycled inputs, infinite loops)

**Example**: A laptop uses $F_{WM} = 0.7$, $L_V = 5$ years, $L_{avg} = 4$ years, $F_R = 0.3$:

$$
MCI = 1 - 0.7 \cdot \frac{5}{2 \cdot 4} + \frac{0.3}{2}
$$

$$
MCI = 1 - 0.7 \cdot 0.625 + 0.15 = 1 - 0.4375 + 0.15 = 0.7125
$$

So the laptop is **71.25 %** circular — a respectable score, but with room for improvement (less virgin material, longer lifespan, more recycled input).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Product Life Cycle — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["STAGE 1: RAW MATERIAL EXTRACTION"]
    s2["STAGE 2: DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT"]
    s3["STAGE 3: MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY"]
    s4["STAGE 4: DISTRIBUTION AND LOGISTICS"]
    s5["STAGE 5: USE PHASE AND SERVICE"]
    s6["STAGE 6: END OF LIFE MANAGEMENT"]
    s7["FINAL DISPOSAL"]
    s8["REUSE LOOP"]
    s9["REMANUFACTURE LOOP"]
    s10["RECYCLE LOOP"]
    s11["ENERGY RECOVERY"]
    s12["CRADLE TO CRADLE FEEDBACK"]
    s13["DESIGN STAGE INPUT FROM EOL DATA"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7

    s6 --> s8
    s6 --> s9
    s6 --> s10
    s6 --> s11

    s8 --> s5
    s9 --> s3
    s10 --> s1
    s11 --> s12

    s6 --> s13
    s13 --> s2
```

> **Diagram Reading Note**: This block-flow renders the *Cradle-to-Grave* spine (s1 → s7) and overlays the **5 R-loops** that constitute *Cradle-to-Cradle* circularity. Each loop shortens the raw-material dependency of subsequent cycles.

---

## 4.2 Service Life Cycle — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sv1["PHASE A: SERVICE STRATEGY AND CONCEPT"]
    sv2["PHASE B: SERVICE DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT"]
    sv3["PHASE C: SERVICE TRANSITION AND LAUNCH"]
    sv4["PHASE D: SERVICE OPERATION AND DELIVERY"]
    sv5["PHASE E: CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT"]
    sv6["PHASE F: SERVICE RETIREMENT"]

    sv1 --> sv2
    sv2 --> sv3
    sv3 --> sv4
    sv4 --> sv5
    sv5 --> sv6

    sv5 --> sv1
    sv6 --> sv2
```

> **Reading Note**: Unlike a product, a **service has no End-of-Life** in the material sense — instead, it undergoes *retirement* or *iteration*. The feedback from Phase E → Phase A makes the Service Life Cycle inherently *iterative* and *agile*, which is why ITIL and ISO 20000 use this loop structure.

---

## 4.3 End-of-Life Decision Hierarchy — 5 R-Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r0["PRODUCT REACHES END OF USEFUL FIRST LIFE"]
    r1["R1: REFUSE OR REDUCE"]
    r2["R2: REUSE"]
    r3["R3: REPAIR OR REFURBISH OR REMANUFACTURE"]
    r4["R4: RECYCLE"]
    r5["R5: RECOVER ENERGY"]
    r6["LANDFILL DISPOSAL"]

    r0 --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 --> r4
    r4 --> r5
    r5 --> r6

    fb["FEEDBACK TO DESIGN STAGE FOR DFE"]
    r2 --> fb
    r3 --> fb
    r4 --> fb
    r5 --> fb
    fb --> r0
```

> **Reading Note**: The diagram obeys the **waste-management hierarchy** codified in EU Directive 2008/98/EC: most-preferred at the top, least-preferred (disposal) at the bottom. The "feedback to design" branch is the key **Design for Environment (DfE)** principle — every EOL outcome must inform next-generation design.

---

## 4.4 Lifecycle Cost — Cumulative Cash-Flow Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["T EQUALS 0: ACQUISITION COST"]
    t1["YEARS 1 TO N: OPERATING COST PER YEAR"]
    t2["YEAR N: END OF LIFE COST"]
    t3["YEAR N: SALVAGE OR RESALE VALUE"]
    t4["LCC: NET PRESENT VALUE OF ALL FLOWS"]

    t0 --> t4
    t1 --> t4
    t2 --> t4
    t3 --> t4
```

> **Reading Note**: This block-flow is the *KTU-favourite* schematic for LCC problems. Always label axes (time in years, cash flow in ₹) when redrawing for the exam.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Define the term "Product Life Cycle". List any four of its stages with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is the sequence of stages a product passes through from raw-material extraction to end-of-life management, typically comprising *Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline* (commercial view) or *Raw Material → Design → Manufacture → Distribution → Use → End-of-Life* (engineering view).

**Four stages (engineering view)**:

1. **Raw Material Acquisition** — Mining and refining of ores, polymers, and bio-mass feedstocks.
2. **Design & Development** — CAD-based engineering, prototyping, and Design-for-Environment (DfE) decisions.
3. **Manufacturing & Assembly** — Transformation of raw materials into finished goods using forming/machining/joining processes.
4. **End-of-Life Management** — Recovery through reuse, remanufacture, recycle, or disposal.

**(3 marks – definitions + 4 stages)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**What are the "5 R-strategies" of End-of-Life (EOL) management? Arrange them in decreasing order of sustainability preference.**

**Model Answer**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **5 R-Strategies of EOL management**, in decreasing order of sustainability preference, are:
>
> 1. **R1 – Refuse / Reduce** *(most preferred)* — Avoid material use altogether or minimise it.
> 2. **R2 – Reuse** — Use the entire product again for the same or different function.
> 3. **R3 – Repair / Refurbish / Remanufacture** — Restore or rebuild components to "as-new" condition.
> 4. **R4 – Recycle** — Reprocess materials at the molecular level into secondary feedstocks.
> 5. **R5 – Recover** *(least preferred among R's)* — Capture energy through controlled incineration.
>
> **Disposal (landfill)** lies *below* R5 and is the absolute last resort.

**(3 marks – 5 Rs listed in order with one-line descriptions)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

#### (a) Explain in detail the six stages of the Product Life Cycle (PLC) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the sustainability considerations at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

The Product Life Cycle comprises six sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Raw Material Acquisition**
- Activities: Mining (e.g., iron ore, lithium, cobalt), refining, agriculture, polymerisation.
- Sustainability issues: Habitat destruction, energy-intensive extraction, conflict minerals, water footprint.
- Engineering lever: Substitute abundant/recycled feedstocks; use bio-based polymers.

**Stage 2 — Design & Development**
- Activities: CAD, CAE simulation, prototyping, Design for X (DfX).
- Sustainability issues: Locked-in footprint (≈70 % of lifecycle impact decided here).
- Engineering lever: **Design for Environment (DfE)**, modular design, design for disassembly, design for recycling.

**Stage 3 — Manufacturing & Production**
- Activities: Casting, forging, machining, moulding, joining, surface treatment, assembly.
- Sustainability issues: Energy intensity, CO₂ emissions, scrap generation, hazardous effluents.
- Engineering lever: Lean manufacturing, renewable-energy-powered plants, closed-loop coolant systems, dry machining.

**Stage 4 — Distribution & Logistics**
- Activities: Packaging, warehousing, multi-modal transport.
- Sustainability issues: Carbon footprint of freight, packaging waste.
- Engineering lever: Modal shift to rail/sea, optimised routing (OR-tools), biodegradable packaging.

**Stage 5 — Use & Service Phase**
- Activities: Operation, maintenance, repair, upgrades.
- Sustainability issues: Energy-in-use, consumables, planned obsolescence.
- Engineering lever: Energy-efficient operation, modular upgrades, predictive maintenance (IoT).

**Stage 6 — End-of-Life (EOL) Management**
- Activities: Collection, sorting, recovery, disposal.
- Sustainability issues: Landfill burden, e-waste toxicity, loss of critical materials.
- Engineering lever: Take-back schemes, reverse logistics, urban mining.

**Neat Block Diagram** *(to be drawn by student in the exam script — see Section 4.1 of these notes for the schematic)*.

**(7 marks: 6 × 1 mark per stage + 1 mark for diagram)**

---

#### (b) Discuss the various End-of-Life (EOL) options for manufactured products. Compare the "Cradle-to-Grave" and "Cradle-to-Cradle" paradigms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**EOL Options** (in order of increasing environmental cost):

1. **Reuse** — Whole product enters second life (e.g., refurbished laptops shipped to schools).
2. **Repair / Refurbish** — Faulty components replaced; cosmetic restoration.
3. **Remanufacture** — Disassembly, cleaning, inspection, replacement of worn parts, reassembly, testing. (e.g., Caterpillar engine remanufacturing — saves up to 85 % material.)
4. **Recycle** — Material-level reprocessing (e.g., aluminium can → new can — uses only 5 % of the energy of primary production).
5. **Energy Recovery** — Controlled incineration with heat-to-power conversion (waste-to-energy).
6. **Disposal** — Landfill or uncontrolled incineration — *least preferred*.

**Cradle-to-Grave (C2G) Paradigm**

$$
\text{Raw Material} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Product} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Waste (Landfill)}
$$

- Linear, "take-make-dispose" model.
- Resources exit the economy forever.
- Dominant in 20th-century industrial models.

**Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) Paradigm**

$$
\text{Nutrients (Biological)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Products} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Nutrients (Technical)}
$$

- Closed loop; outputs of one process become *inputs* of another.
- Two material metabolisms: **biological** (compostable) and **technical** (recyclable).
- Inspired by nature's nutrient cycles; championed by William McDonough & Michael Braungart.

**Comparative Table** *(to be drawn by student)*:

| Aspect | Cradle-to-Grave | Cradle-to-Cradle |
|---|---|---|
| Resource use | Virgin only | Virgin + Recycled |
| Waste output | High | Near-zero |
| Design philosophy | End-of-pipe | Inherently closed-loop |
| Energy source | Fossil dominant | Renewable preferred |
| Economic model | Linear "throwaway" | Circular / service-based |
| Industry example | Single-use plastic | Fairphone modular design |

**(7 marks: 3 marks EOL options + 2 marks C2G + 2 marks C2C comparison)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

#### (a) Describe the Service Life Cycle (SLC). How does it differ from the Product Life Cycle (PLC)? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Service Life Cycle (SLC)** — the structured journey of a service offering from concept to retirement. Drawing on ITIL V4 and ISO 20000:

1. **Service Strategy** — Define market, value proposition, assets, and portfolio.
2. **Service Design** — Architect the service: SLAs, processes, technology, metrics.
3. **Service Transition** — Move the service from development into live operation (testing, training, release).
4. **Service Operation** — Day-to-day delivery and support.
5. **Continual Service Improvement (CSI)** — Iterative optimisation based on customer feedback and metrics.
6. **Service Retirement** — Decommissioning when obsolete or replaced.

**Key Differences between SLC and PLC** *(to be tabulated by student)*:

| Aspect | Product Life Cycle | Service Life Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Tangible good | Intangible offering |
| **Production & Consumption** | Separated in time and space | Simultaneous (inseparable) |
| **Storage** | Possible (inventory) | Perishable — cannot be stored |
| **Quality variability** | Low (standardised) | High (heterogeneous, person-dependent) |
| **Customer role** | Passive buyer | Co-producer |
| **End-of-Life concept** | Physical disposal / recovery | Service retirement / replacement |
| **Iteration frequency** | One-pass per generation | Continuous (CSI loop) |
| **Primary sustainability metric** | Material circularity (MCI) | Service efficiency / utilisation |
| **Engineering discipline** | Mechanical/industrial design | Service engineering / operations research |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: A service's "life" can be extended almost indefinitely via continual improvement, while a product's life is bounded by material fatigue and technological obsolescence.

**(7 marks: 4 marks SLC phases + 3 marks comparison)**

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of **Cradle-to-Cradle Design** with suitable industrial examples. Discuss the role of **Design for Environment (DfE)** in achieving lifecycle sustainability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) Design Philosophy**

Coined by **William McDonough and Michael Braungart** (2002), C2C design reimagines all manufactured products as participants in one of two material metabolisms:

- **Biological Metabolism** — Materials (e.g., cotton, bioplastics, wood) safely re-enter the biosphere as nutrients.
- **Technical Metabolism** — Materials (e.g., aluminium, polymers, rare earths) circulate indefinitely within closed-loop industrial cycles.

**Three Core Principles of C2C**:

1. **Waste = Food** — Every output is an input for another process.
2. **Use Renewable Energy** — Powered by solar, wind, hydro, geothermal.
3. **Celebrate Diversity** — Cultural, biological, and material diversity enriches resilience.

**Industrial Examples**:

| Industry | Company | C2C Initiative |
|---|---|---|
| Apparel | **Patagonia** | Worn Wear programme — take back, repair, resell used garments |
| Electronics | **Fairphone** | Modular phone with replaceable modules; recycled-content materials |
| Flooring | **Shaw Industries** | Cradle-to-Cradle certified carpets using recycled PVC |
| FMCG | **Unilever** | "Less, better, longer" framework across detergent and shampoo ranges |
| Automotive | **Renault** | "Choisy-le-Roi" factory remanufactures 70+ vehicle components |

**Design for Environment (DfE)**

DfE is a systematic design methodology that embeds environmental considerations into every design decision, with 8 cardinal guidelines:

1. Design for **material efficiency** (minimise mass and toxicity).
2. Design for **disassembly** (snap-fits > adhesives > welds).
3. Design for **remanufacturing** (modularity, accessibility).
4. Design for **recycling** (mono-material, marked resins).
5. Design for **durability** (extended product life).
6. Design for **energy efficiency in use**.
7. Design for **renewable / recycled input materials**.
8. Design for **safe EOL handling** (non-hazardous separation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DfE is the Keystone**: Approximately **70–80 %** of a product's lifecycle environmental impact is *fixed* at the design stage. Once manufactured, locked-in decisions (material choice, joining methods, modularity) cannot be reversed. DfE is therefore the highest-leverage point in lifecycle management.

**(7 marks: 3 marks C2C philosophy + 2 marks examples + 2 marks DfE guidelines)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Lifecycle Questions — Direct Loss of Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Product Life Cycle (marketing) with Product Life Cycle (engineering).** The marketing PLC (Intro–Growth–Maturity–Decline) is *commercial*; the engineering PLC (Raw Material → EOL) is *technical*. In **PESFT631 / Sustainable Manufacturing**, examiners expect the *engineering* interpretation unless the question specifies "commercial sales curve".
>
> 2. **Mixing percentage and decimal forms of discount rate (r).** Always use **r = 0.08 for 8 %**, never 8. Sketches that show 8 inside the formula lose 1 full mark.
>
> 3. **Omitting units in final answer.** Always write "₹8,57,196" or "71.25 %" — never just a bare number.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign for salvage value / resale revenue** in LCC formula. The salvage value is a *cash inflow* and must be **subtracted** (or treated as a negative cost).
>
> 5. **Drawing the lifecycle as a flat linear sequence when loops are present.** Examiners explicitly look for the **5 R-loops** in any EOL diagram; a linear-only depiction forfeits 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Service vs Product distinction.** In service-lifecycle questions, students often list product stages verbatim — examiners mark *zero* for this. Always differentiate by using ITIL/ISO 20000 terminology.
>
> 7. **Writing LCC formula without discount factor (1+r)^i.** Even if the numerical answer accidentally matches, the missing discount factor forfeits 2 marks because it indicates conceptual misunderstanding.
>
> 8. **Bass Diffusion — confusing p and q.** $p$ = innovation, $q$ = imitation. Swapping these inverts the curve shape and the answer becomes numerically wrong by orders of magnitude.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lifecycle of Products/Services**

### A. Conceptual Pillars (must know verbatim)

- **PLC engineering stages**: Raw Material → Design → Manufacturing → Distribution → Use → EOL (6 stages).
- **PLC commercial stages**: Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline (4 stages — distinct from engineering).
- **5 R's in correct order**: Refuse/Reduce ➡ Reuse ➡ Repair/Refurbish/Remanufacture ➡ Recycle ➡ Recover ➡ Disposal.
- **C2G vs C2C**: Open loop vs closed loop (biological + technical nutrients).
- **DfE** = 8 guidelines that lock in 70–80 % of lifecycle impact at the design stage.
- **Service uniqueness**: Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability.

### B. Numerical & Formula Anchors

- **Bass Diffusion**: $N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p}e^{-(p+q)t}}$ — **p** innovation, **q** imitation, **m** market potential.
- **LCC**: $LCC = C_{acq} + \sum \frac{C_{op,i}}{(1+r)^i} + \frac{C_{EOL}}{(1+r)^n} - \frac{R_{res}}{(1+r)^n}$.
- **Recycling Rate**: $\eta_{recycle} = \frac{M_{recycled}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$.
- **Recovery Rate**: $\eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{total,EOL}} \times 100\%$.
- **MCI**: $MCI = 1 - F_{WM}\cdot\frac{L_V}{2L_{avg}} + \frac{F_R}{2}$, range [0, 1].
- **Embodied Energy**: $EE_{total} = \sum_j M_j \cdot EE_j$.
- **Logistics Carbon Footprint**: $CF = \sum_k m_k \cdot d_k \cdot EF_k$.

### C. Industrial Exemplars (use in 14-mark answers)

- **Patagonia** (apparel C2C), **Fairphone** (modular electronics), **Caterpillar** (remanufacturing), **Renault Choisy-le-Roi** (auto reman), **Apple Daisy** (e-waste), **Shaw Industries** (C2C-certified flooring), **Unilever** (FMCG lifecycle), **Volkswagen ELV** (95 % recovery target).

### D. KTU Vocabulary to Use in Answers

- "**Cradle-to-Cradle**" not "recycling"
- "**Design for Environment (DfE)**" not "eco-design" (though equivalent in some texts)
- "**5 R-hierarchy**" not "waste management"
- "**Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)**" not "circularity score"
- "**Reverse Logistics**" not "take-back scheme"
- "**Remanufacture**" ≠ "Refurbish" ≠ "Repair" — distinguish these three precisely.

### E. Frequently Tested Concepts in KTU 2024 Scheme

1. ✅ 6 engineering stages of PLC with one-line description.
2. ✅ 5 R-strategies in correct hierarchical order.
3. ✅ Cradle-to-Grave vs Cradle-to-Cradle comparison.
